issue_title: Underline characters ignored in string values
issue_body:
Using the latest git commit, it seems that underline characters in string values are being ignored:

```python
>>> toml.loads('x="a_b"')
{'x': 'ab'}
```

I don't think that I'm missing some escape character, because if I start from a valid python string, then dump it into toml format, and then parse it back, the underline characters are still lost:

```python
>>> toml.loads(toml.dumps({'x': 'a_b'}))
{'x': 'ab'}
```

At the moment dumps does not care about the order of the items in the passed dictionary.  However, when passing an ordered mapping like ``collections.OrderedDictionary`` it should probably try to preserve the order order in the outputted string as well.

Would it be possible to preserve the comments while reading a toml file and write the comments back while dumping it? 

This would be tremendously useful for tools that manage their config files and write out the toml in an automatic fashion.

issue_title: Port doesn't update on scheme change
issue_body:
If I create a scheme-less `furl` object with no explicit port set and then at a later date set the scheme to one which has a known default port, it doesn't update the `port` attribute. For example:

```python
from furl import furl

print(furl('http://example.com').port)
print(furl('unknown://example.com').set(scheme='http').port)
print(furl().set(netloc='example.com').set(scheme='http').port)
```

I get 80, `None`, `None`, whereas I'd expect 80 each time.

For linux users (I'm one) default selector is epoll, for mac/bsd folks it is kqueue.
epoll works with sockets. But it gives error for regular files. If you fall back to poll or
select it 'seems' they work but in fact these selectors always report readiness.
If you go ahead, you'll block. The blocking is expected to be 'short' but it may
not be the case. So Curio uses threads to avoid blocking. That's the current situation.

I wonder if curio can take advantage of kqueue facilities on supported platforms.
I read that one can give anything with a file descriptor to kqueue. Even timers
are natively supported. Does taking advantage of such facilities mean a special
fork of Curio kernel? Current I'm not a user of those platforms -but thinking about it.

So this issue is created for discussing platform specific selectors Curio
may or may not use.

I have an issue with converting between same units with different magnitude >>>ur = UnitRegistry()
>>>ur.parse_expression('1*cm').to(ur.parse_expression('10*cm'))
\ I would expect for the answer to be 10 centimeters

When executing certain conversions, it's possible to get results that differ between subsequent Python sessions. The differences are a result of floating point math being non-associative. If unit conversion factors are multiplied in a different order, small floating point errors can change direction. An example of this phenomenon is shown below:
```python
In [1]: 200 * 1e-3 / 1e-9
Out[1]: 200000000.0

In [2]: 1 / 1e-9 * 200 * 1e-3
Out[2]: 199999999.99999997
```
Pint essentially stores units in a dictionary (the UnitContainer object), and since the keys in such a dictionary are unordered, the order in which they will be multiplied during a conversion is non-deterministic. The following short script will exhibit different results as they are run in subsequent Python sessions.
```python
# pint_bug.py
from pint import UnitRegistry
units = UnitRegistry()

u1 = units.carat
u2 = units.ng
print([k for k in (u1._units / u2._units)])
print((1 * u1).to(u2).magnitude)
```
Running the test script multiple times
```bash
$ python pint_bug.py
['carat', 'nanogram']
200000000.0
$ python pint_bug.py
['nanogram', 'carat']
199999999.99999997
```
While there may not be a good solution for eliminating this floating point math error, I think we should at least make the unit conversion order deterministic to invoke the least surprise (and pain) upon users.

issue_title: Add global document index in admin interface
Trim allows start time without an end
issue_body: Trim can trim a file from the beginning with only the start time (e.g. `sox input output trim 10`) but pysox's trim requires both start and end. Added #81 as a fix.

issue_title: Consider replacing string formatting with babel.dates.format_datetime
issue_body: Zulu uses the same Unicode date patterns as Babel does but Babel has locale support. Should be a drop-in replacement with the added benefit of supporting a locale option in `zulu.DateTime.format()`.

issue_title: Line length calculation is dependent on line endings
issue_body:
```
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Run pyang --ietf on the attached testLF.yang:

% pyang --ietf testLF.yang 
testLF.yang:1: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 71 exceeds 70 
characters
testLF.yang:2: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 72 exceeds 70 
characters
testLF.yang:3: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 73 exceeds 70 
characters
...

(note that testLF.yang has "Unix" LF line endings)

2. Run pyang --ietf on the attached testCRLF.yang:

% pyang --ietf testCRLF.yang 
testCRLF.yang:1: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 72 exceeds 70 
characters
testCRLF.yang:2: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 73 exceeds 70 
characters
testCRLF.yang:3: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 74 exceeds 70 
characters
...

(note that testCRLF.yang has "DOS" CR LF line endings)

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?

By my calculation, these lines are of length 70, 71 and 72, so I would expect 
to see the following for both of them:

testCRLF.yang:2: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 71 exceeds 70 
characters
testCRLF.yang:3: warning: IETF rule (RFC formatting): line length 72 exceeds 70 
characters

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?

Latest SVN version on OS X Yosemite.

Please provide any additional information below.

It looks to me as though this behaviour stems from text.splitlines(True) in 
yang_parser.py below:

class YangTokenizer(object):
    def __init__(self, text, pos, errors,
                 max_line_len=None, keep_comments=False):
        self.lines = collections.deque(text.splitlines(True))

This is retaining line endings, which will be either LF (adding 1 to the line 
length) or CR LF (adding 2 to the line length).

So the fix might be as simple as changing the above to text.splitlines(False)?
```

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `william....@gmail.com` on 20 Jan 2015 at 10:12

Attachments:
* [test.zip](https://storage.googleapis.com/google-code-attachments/pyang/issue-122/comment-0/test.zip)

issue_title: problem of rendering a template file
issue_body:
Hi : 
     I'm seeking for a solution of how to populate a pdf template file and finally found [one which using pdfrw](https://bostata.com/post/how_to_populate_fillable_pdfs_with_python/) .That example is quite simple and straightforward by using `adobe acrobat` as template editing tool and `python dict` as a render way.
   But it has its own problems with the result pdf file:
  * the content of each cell rendered cannot be seen until they got manually clicked .(**serious**)
  * the `checkbox` element in pdf cannot be rendered. (**middle**)
  * the cell is not freezed , and anyone who opened the file can edit it freely.(**low**)

   I've tried to investigate , but it seems requires plenty of knowledge about canvas and pdf format which is boring and time consuming . 
  So , is there any guys who has experienecs of doing things like this ?  Appreciate a lot if you don't mind to share .

Hi

Thanks for writing this useful library! I am trying it from a few days and felt the need for better support for non HTS labels.

It would be good to have something like this: https://github.com/facebookresearch/loop/blob/master/utils.py#L143 which does not depend on label files and uses `nltk` 's cmudict to generate phonemes.

I can contribute if you guide me.

My current workaround is that I use merlin's scripts to generate test and train labels to use with your code.

Hi,

Yesterday I had the unpleasant surprise to find my app non-working after the update of pysodium to 0.6.9.
After a bit I found that the introduction of the aead* function was the root of the problem.
I'm on FreeBSD and the libsodium package is still 1.0.8 (aead* functions where introduced in 1.0.9).

So we have two possibility : 
1) Check the libsodium version at runtime and raise something if we need an upgraded version
2) Only import function from libsodium based on the version

I will happily provide a patch depending on which way you prefer.

Cheers,

issue_title: Options set via config file are disrespected
issue_body:
Options set via a config file are no longer working for me. Here's a simple example:

    $ cat ~/.green
    verbose = 2

    $ green
    .....
    Ran 5 tests in 0.001s
    OK (passes=5)

I observe the same problem with other options in my config file (eg, `file_pattern`). They aren't being picked up at all.

When I checkout to v1.71, the example above works as expected, but not in v1.80 and beyond.

ZAP was started : zap-cli --zap-path /opt/ZAP_2.7.0/ -p 8090 --api-key 12345 start
I tried to import my context , but firstly I got:
[ERROR]           Importing context from file failed: Internal Error
After that I got: 
[ERROR]           Importing context from file failed: The external data provided is not valid.

My context uses script auth. How can I create script for zap-cli ?
I can't write PDF file in cyrillic.

Hi,

Is it possible to set the documentclass? For instance, I want to use the Springer layout. In LaTeX I would write this:

    \documentclass[runningheads]{llncs}

Where there is an `llncs.cls` style file.

But there are lots of different style, e.g. the IEEE format.

Thanks,

Laszlo

issue_title: Entity registrations are not reflected in home assistant
issue_body:
If a light/switch/sensor is added to the deconz network while home assistant is already running; it doesn't get reported. Home-assistant needs to be restarted for it to recognize the newly added component.

This is particularly painful, if deconz and home-assistant are started one after the other. Because the deconz rest plugin does not report any lights unless it has been discovered first. (even if they are already registered in the db to belong to the network) As a result, any component that was not discovered yet during startup, will never show on home-assistant.

issue_title: CUDA not being use with python API
issue_body:
Hi 
I compiled the newest version of dlib (straight from master) with VS 2015. I used your setup.py script and I'm sure that during that process the info about that CUDA will be used was displayed. I have CUDA 9 installed with cuDNN 7. Now I don't see any gpu use while using function like compute_face_descriptor. 
Edit: Log of process of compilation and setup https://pastebin.com/x6B8nSuq

issue_title: can you give an example for adding new metric learning layers?
issue_body:
There is a new metric learning about DNN, in CVPR2017, 
'https://github.com/wy1iu/sphereface'

I am going to add a layer of sphereface model to train my own face recogintion,
but there are too many errors.
can you add the sphere layer and give an example for training.

issue_title: dnn mmod with varying aspect ratios of a similar object
issue_body:
Hi!
I am trying to implement the front and back car detector for traffic signs. The problem is that sometimes signs are facing the camera (with a square aspect ratio) and sometimes are seen from the side (as a rectangle).

Since I saw the detector is having a hard time training (and gave a recall of 0), and since it was hard to tell the limit of when a sign from the side is different enough to constitute a different class, I decided to just train a separate "side view" detector by just going through the xml file and adding ignore = '1' to each label that is closely a square.

I keep getting this message (with varying values for height and width, that as a side note do not actually exist in my xml label file):

"Warning, ignoring object.  We encountered a truth rectangle with a width and height of 171 and 166.  The image pyramid and sliding windows can't output a rectangle of this shape.  This is either because (1) the final layer's features have too large of a stride across the image, limiting the possible locations the sliding window can search or (2) because the rectangle's aspect ratio is too different from the best matching detection window, which has a width and height of 30 and 65."

Does this refer to the ignored boxes or is it another mistake I missed?

Thanks!

Hello,  yesterday I have downloaded latest MSVC 2015 Update 3.
Compiler version is: 19.00.24215.1

And I have successfully compiled all DNN examples (CPU mode only) and also test_for_cpp11 compiles well too

To build examples I put explicit flag:
`cmake .. -DCOMPILER_CAN_DO_CPP_11=ON`

And imagenet exmples - both example and trainer are not compiling well - compiler still goes into infinite loop. But previous versions were unable to compile dnn_introduction2_ex. Now it compiles as-is

I have tried to find the reason why does it not compile and looks like DNN structure is too-templated for MSVC.
I made small changes and it compiled well:

```
template <typename SUBNET> using level1 = ares<512,ares<512,ares_down<512,SUBNET>>>;
template <typename SUBNET> using level2 = ares<256,ares<256,ares<256,ares<256,ares<256,ares_down<256,SUBNET>>>>>>;
template <typename SUBNET> using level3 = ares<128,ares<128,ares<128,ares_down<128,SUBNET>>>>;
template <typename SUBNET> using level4 = ares<64,ares<64,ares<64,SUBNET>>>;
using anet_type = loss_multiclass_log<fc<1000,avg_pool_everything<
                            level1<
                            level2<
                            level3<
                            level4<
                            max_pool<3,3,2,2,relu<affine<con<64,7,7,2,2,
                            input_rgb_image_sized<227>
                            >>>>>>>>>>>;

issue_title: Append changeset hash to version if it's not tagged
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Anonymous**

----------------------------------------

At the moment, hgtools returns a version like N.N.Ndev when getting the version name of a changeset that isn't tagged. This means that multiple changesets will be given the same version name, making the name less meaningful than it could be.

Git's describe function (by default) produces something like the following when called on a commit without a tag:

<latest-tag>-<commits-since-tag>-g<partial-commit-hash>

e.g., 1.4.3-3-g344b483

Because of this, every commit is almost guaranteed to have a unique version name, which is much more useful. It'd be useful if hgtools provided something similar.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/hgtools/issue/15

issue_title: Error on keyring password get/set shouldn't disrupt user action
issue_body:
Currently, if an error occurs on password setting or retrieval that causes an exception to be raised, the user action is interrupted.

This is impractical; if there's anything strange with the keyring, the user should be allowed to just go on working by manually entering the password, and shouldn't need to disable the keyring extensions at all in order to proceed.

Also fixes a condition in the current testcases where the test would randomly work because it did not really set a keyring before being run - i.e. the test success was dependent on the order the tests were run. I had to fix this in order to add a unit test for my enhancement.

new feature?

Gmaps bundles useful GeoJSON geometries to save users the work of having to find them from the Internet. There are currently a [handful](https://github.com/pbugnion/gmaps/blob/master/gmaps/geojson_geometries/geojson_geometries.py) of geometries included. Adding new geometries is a very useful way to contribute to `gmaps`.
If you use gmaps with your own GeoJSON file, chances are that other people would also find it useful. Contributing the file back to gmaps is a meaningful way to contribute to the project.

## What makes a good GeoJSON file

- GeoJSON files should be between 1MB and 3MB. If your file is larger than that, you can use [mapshaper](http://www.mapshaper.org) to simplify it. 

- The license needs to match that of GMaps: the file should either be in the public domain, or it should be MIT or Apache licensed.

## How to contribute GeoJSON files

If you have a way to share your file on the Internet (either by putting it on Amazon S3, or through a Dropbox link, or by putting it on a web server), then do this and add the link to the [geojson_geometries](https://github.com/pbugnion/gmaps/blob/master/gmaps/geojson_geometries/geojson_geometries.py)) module. We will probably copy the file onto the Amazon S3 bucket stored with gmaps and change the link that you submitted.

If you do not have a way to share the file, open an issue in GitHub and upload your file through that.

in `scapy/layers/inet6.py`, the function `getmacbyip6()` ends with the following:

```
    res = neighsol(ip6, a, iff, chainCC=chainCC)

    if res is not None:
        if ICMPv6NDOptDstLLAddr in res:
            mac = res[ICMPv6NDOptDstLLAddr].lladdr
    else:
        mac = res.src
        conf.netcache.in6_neighbor[ip6] = mac
        return mac

    return None
```

This doesn't make sense.  in the stanza following `if` statement, `mac` is set but never used (and `None` is returned).  In the `else` stanza -- where `res` must be `None` -- it references a subfield of `res` , which is guaranteed to fail.

Something like "if obj.path.split('/')[2][1:-1] in selected_channels:" ( I have not taken into account the group because my channels have only unique identifiers)

I have forked your library and I have implemented this condition. But I cannot run it because the read function of types.py shows me this exception "Python int too large to convert to C long" when running timedelta(seconds=seconds)

If I run the library from the site-packages.nptdms it works correctly unless I execute step by step (I do it with eclipse) that it also throws me this exception. Do you know what it can be?

issue_title: saveas does not work with non-bed output - bug or feature?
issue_body:
I was doing some `groupby` calculations returning a two column results, but saving them with `.saveas()` returns an error:

> Traceback (most recent call last):
>   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 668, in decorated
>     result = method(self, *args, **kwargs)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 2729, in saveas
>     fn = self._collapse(self, fn=fn, trackline=trackline)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 1097, in _collapse
>     for i in iterable:
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 924, in __iter__
>     elif self.file_type == 'sam':
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pybedtools/bedtool.py", line 857, in file_type
>     self._file_type = IntervalFile(self.fn).file_type
>   File "pybedtools/cbedtools.pyx", line 694, in pybedtools.cbedtools.IntervalFile.file_type (pybedtools/cbedtools.cpp:10001)
>   File "pybedtools/cbedtools.pyx", line 695, in pybedtools.cbedtools.IntervalFile.file_type (pybedtools/cbedtools.cpp:9954)
>   File "pybedtools/cbedtools.pyx", line 529, in pybedtools.cbedtools.create_interval_from_list (pybedtools/cbedtools.cpp:7272)
> IndexError: list index out of range

Saving the result with `output=` works fine. My guess is that `.saveas()` tries to validate the output as the bed file, but `output=` doesn't (sorry couldn't find the source code to see if this is correct). Here is a reproducible example:

```python
# Python 2.7.6 (default, Jun 22 2015, 17:58:13) 
# [GCC 4.8.2] on linux2
# Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

import pybedtools
# pybedtools 0.6.9

a = pybedtools.example_bedtool('gdc.gff')
b = pybedtools.example_bedtool('gdc.bed')
c = a.intersect(b, c=True)
print 
d = c.groupby(g=[1, 4, 5], c=10, ops=['sum'])
print d
## works
d.saveas('test1.tmp')

## now 2 col output
print c.groupby(g=[3], c=10, ops=['sum'])
## fails
c.groupby(g=[3], c=10, ops=['sum']).saveas('test2.tmp')
## works
c.groupby(g=[3], c=10, ops=['sum'], output='test2.tmp')
``` 

This might be a feature, but I couldn't find it in the docs.

Cheers
Is it possible to convert bam to 2 strand specific bigwig files, if strand orientations are given for strand specific experiments?

issue_title: process.create_time() and hash(process) are not immutable on linux
issue_body:
I've run into two related issues using psutil on linux (Ubuntu 14.04.3):
1. Because of the way Process.create_time() is calculated it changes if the system time changes (commonly from ntp corrections, but in my instance I noticed it because the clock changed during the boot sequence).  This may or may not be a big deal depending on what you want the creation time for.
2. The documentation states that hash(process) is calculated from the pid and the creation_time, thus if the creation_time changes the hash also changes.  This is a big deal since it defeats the point of a hash.

The problem does not appear to be present on Windows, presumably due to the way creation_time is calculated.

To reproduce:
1) Pick a process, e.g. 12345
2) Start python from the command line (Note: the actual values are just for example purposes)

``` python
import psutil
p = psutil.Process(12345)
p.creation_time()
>>>1447426717.05
hash(p)
>>>10728053
```

3) Change the date: date -s "Fri Nov 13 10:09:44 EST 2015"
4) Start python again (Note: it is important to restart python since psutil caches the value BASE_TIME which it uses to calculate create_time)

``` python
import psutil
p = psutil.Process(12345)
p.creation_time()
>>><creation time will be different>
hash(p)
>>><hash will be different>
```

I do not know what the solution to calculating creation_time is, however, the hash could be made immutable by using the "start time in jiffies since system boot" value (value[19] from https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/5df7474cf1fcd5cb15c22c255aa55568aa5fd071/psutil/_pslinux.py#L913) directly rather than the full create_time, since that appears to be constant.

issue_title: Faked time shouldn't always be the same.
issue_body:
Excerpt from the docs:
"[…] all calls to ``datetime.datetime.now()``, ``datetime.datetime.utcnow()``, […] will return the time that has been frozen."

But this is not the behaviour I expect.

Example code:
```python
from datetime import datetime

def test_something():
    print('now():   ', datetime.now())
    print('utcnow():', datetime.utcnow())
```

Output without Freezegun (system timezone is "Europe/Berlin"):
```
now():    2015-03-10 00:46:40.465879
utcnow(): 2015-03-09 23:46:40.466031
```

Output with Freezegun (method decorated with ``@freeze_time('2012-03-11 19:21:23')``):
```
now():    2012-03-11 19:21:23
utcnow(): 2012-03-11 19:21:23
```

However, I expected the output with Freezegun to reflect the offset (one hour, in this case) and, thus, to return two different date/time instances.

issue_title: Support Zookeeper offset storage in KafkaConsumer
issue_body:
Specific underlying feature related to #706 and #737 . Zookeeper offset storage is flagged by the protocol version used w/ OffsetCommit and OffsetFetch. But also zookeeper offset storage does not use a group coordinator node (any node will respond to commit/fetch requests). This means that we also need to avoid group coordinator node identification code / error checking.

Ideally we should be able to automatically enable zookeeper offsets when using broker versions < 0.9, and kafka offsets when >= 0.9

issue_title: on_partitions_revoked called with all partitions, not only the ones being revoked
issue_body:
When kafka is rebalancing the partitions, the `on_partitions_revoked` method will be called for all assigned partitions, not only the onces that are revoked. This was reproduced in kafka-python 1.3.2.

According to the documentation, a use case of this function is to be able to flush cached data for a partition that is no longer assigned. With the current behavior such function would flush the whole cache, even if there where only a new partition being assigned.

~I looked at the code and came up with a possible solution. I'm new to this code, so there might be something that I have missed, like what happens if the join fails.~

~1. Store the currently assigned partitions in `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_prepare`.
2. Move the call to `on_partitions_revoked` from `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_prepare` to `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_complete`.
3. Diff the previous and the new assigned partitions in `ConsumerCoordinator._on_join_complete` and call `on_partitions_revoked` and `on_partitions_assigned` with the correct partitions.~

EDIT:
After working some more with other lower level libraries, I think that calling `on_partitions_revoked` with all partitions is probably the correct thing to do, What could be done is to clarify this in the documentation.

issue_title: Data Loss caused by StopIteration in message deserializer
issue_body:
A bug in the fastavro package for python raises "StopIteration" when deserializing truncated messages. kafka-python sees the StopIteration and aborts the loop when consumer a batch of messages. This results in the loss of data with no warning or exceptions being thrown.

While the root cause is a bug in an unrelated package, kafka-python should raise an exception when the deserializer class behaves unexpectedly. This was tested against version '0.9.5', and a quick glance at master suggest it has not been resolved.

See also: https://github.com/tebeka/fastavro/issues/46

issue_title: Binding many parameters to an insert statement causes a memory leak
issue_body:
### Environment
- Python: 3.6.4
- pyodbc: 4.0.22
- OS: Ubuntu 16.04.4 LTS
- DB: Microsoft SQL Server 2016 (RTM-CU2) (KB3182270) - 13.0.2164.0 (X64) 
	Sep  9 2016 20:13:26 
	Copyright (c) Microsoft Corporation
	Standard Edition (64-bit) on Windows Server 2012 R2 Standard 6.3 <X64> (Build 9600: ) (Hypervisor)
- driver: unixODBC 2.3.1

### Issue

Inserting rows using a query with many bound parameters causes a memory leak.

example code:

python```
@contextmanager
def get_cursor():
    connection = pyodbc.connect(CONNECTION_STRING)
    cursor = connection.cursor()
    try:
        yield cursor
    except pyodbc.DatabaseError as err:
        logger.exception(err)
    finally:
        connection.close()
data = ['bla', 'bla', 'bla', ..., 'bla']
query = """INSERT INTO foo (bar, zam, zip, ... , qux) VALUES ?,?,?, ... ,?"""
for i in range(100000):
    with get_cursor as cursor():
        cursor.execute(query, *data)
```

I ran similar code using `tracemalloc` and the line with `cursor.execute` was the culprit of the memory leak. Escaping the values manually and adding them to the query string fixed the leak.

[Possible related issue](https://code.google.com/archive/p/pyodbc/issues/145)

### Environment

To diagnose, we usually need to know the following, including version numbers.  On Windows, be
sure to specify 32-bit Python or 64-bit:

- Python: 3.5
- pyodbc: 4.0.22
- OS: Debian 9.0 (stretch)
- DB: MSSQL 2012
- driver: {ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server}

This issue discusses ways we can speed up continuous integration by being a bit selective about what we include. This seems like it would particularly useful for @santoshphilip while developing the `py90dot1` project, as well as for any other contributors. Continuous integration is great, but when it's slow it becomes a lot less useful as immediate feedback.

My suggestions:

1. Remove the OSX builds from continuous integration entirely

They can take a couple of hours to run on Travis, while the Linux builds there are done in under five minutes. Windows on Appveyor comes in just under 30 minutes. Since @santoshphilip and I both develop on OSX, this is also the least likely place for us to miss a platform-specific issue.

2. Only test the most recent release version of Python 2 and Python 3, and of EnergyPlus

We currently cover 2.7, 3.5 and 3.6 for EnergyPlus versions 8.6, 8.7 and 8.8 (just 8.7 and 8.8 on Appveyor). Removing specific support for Python 3.5, and EnergyPlus 8.5 and 8.6 would reduce the Appveyor testing time in particular since jobs run one after another on that platform. By only testing the most recent versions, we could expect to get the time down from 30 minutes to around 10.

3. Run comprehensive tests for releases

We can make a way to only run OSX builds and builds for older versions of Python and EnergyPlus when merging into `master`. Then the only time we'd need to wait for hours on CI would be when preparing to merge `develop` into `master` for a release, or for any hotfixes that may need to go straight to `master`.

issue_body:
I'm not quite shure if it's an actual bug or a missunderstanding on my side.

If we look at the main part of the [IXXATBus.recv() method](https://github.com/hardbyte/python-can/blob/develop/can/interfaces/ixxat/canlib.py#L387), mainly at the part which handles the timeout != 0 case ([Line 408 ff](https://github.com/hardbyte/python-can/blob/develop/can/interfaces/ixxat/canlib.py#L408)):

```python
# Wait if no message available
t0 = _timer_function()
elapsed_ms = 0
remaining_ms = 0
while elapsed_ms <= timeout:
	elapsed_ms = int((_timer_function() - t0) * 1000)
	remaining_ms = timeout - elapsed_ms
	# Wait until at least one frame is in the buffer
	try:
		_canlib.canChannelWaitRxEvent(self._channel_handle, remaining_ms)
	except VCITimeout:
		log.debug('canChannelWaitRxEvent timed out after %dms', remaining_ms)
		return None

	# In theory we should be fine with a 0 timeout since the rxEvent was already
	# set but I've seen timeouts appearing here and there
	try:
		_canlib.canChannelReadMessage(self._channel_handle, 0, ctypes.byref(self._message))
	except VCITimeout:
		continue

	# See if we got a data or info/error messages
	if self._message.uMsgInfo.Bits.type == constants.CAN_MSGTYPE_DATA:
		tm = _timer_function()
		break

	elif self._message.uMsgInfo.Bits.type == constants.CAN_MSGTYPE_INFO:
		log.info(CAN_INFO_MESSAGES.get(self._message.abData[0], "Unknown CAN info message code {}".format(self._message.abData[0])))

	elif self._message.uMsgInfo.Bits.type == constants.CAN_MSGTYPE_ERROR:
		log.warning(CAN_ERROR_MESSAGES.get(self._message.abData[0], "Unknown CAN error message code {}".format(self._message.abData[0])))

	elif self._message.uMsgInfo.Bits.type == constants.CAN_MSGTYPE_TIMEOVR:
		pass
	else:
		log.warn("Unexpected message info type")
```

Now consider multiple messages arrive very fast and a RxEvent occours. We start processing the first message, but it happens to be a CAN info or error message. This is fine, we just ignore the data and return to the top of the loop, where we wait for the next RxEvent. In my understanding `canChannelWaitRxEvent` will timeout despite having data in the Queue, therefore `None` is returned instead of a new message.

The same would happen if multiple messages arrive very fast and one readout cycle lasts longer than the time between two messages.

I might be wrong, so please correct me if the behaviour is not as described. If someone can confirm this behaviour, I can provide a fix for this.

### Test case
```python
import asyncio, asynctnt
import asynctnt.exceptions
import os


def sh(cmd):
    return asyncio.get_running_loop().run_in_executor(None, os.system, cmd)


async def run():
    c = asynctnt.Connection(reconnect_timeout=1, connect_timeout=1, request_timeout=1)
    await sh('docker run --rm --name tt -d -p 3301:3301 tarantool/tarantool:1.10.0')
    print('started Tarantool')

    await c.connect()
    print('connected')
    await c.call('box.info')
    print('first call ok')

    await sh('docker rm -fv tt')
    print('stopped Tarantool')

    await sh('docker run --rm --name tt -d -p 3301:3301 tarantool/tarantool:1.10.0')
    print('started Tarantool')

    for _ in range(10):
        try:
            if _ % 2 == 0:
                # The following two lines don't change anything if uncommented
                await c.connect()
                print('re-connected')

            await c.call('box.info')
            print('subsequent call ok')  # <- This fails when Tarantool is back up
        except asynctnt.exceptions.TarantoolNotConnectedError as e:
            print('subsequent call error:', e)
        await asyncio.sleep(1)


try:
    print('asynctnt', asynctnt.__version__)
    asyncio.run(run())
finally:
    os.system('docker rm -fv tt')
```

### Output:
```
asynctnt 0.1.13
a1d11906a6e915a19dbf4efed2a5c8f561f4905c587f5e8331579912a55c79e3
started Tarantool
connected
first call ok
Connect to Tarantool[127.0.0.1:3301] failed: CancelledError(). Retrying in 1.000000 seconds
tt
stopped Tarantool
4d568b320b62d10d9053407689dd27b20ff4abd41bb73e3c8c1b6e964878a57e
started Tarantool
re-connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
re-connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
re-connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
re-connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
re-connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
subsequent call error: Tarantool is not connected
tt
```

### Expected behavior

I expect Asynctnt to try to reconnect under the hood when the subsequent `call()` s are executed without requiring a user to `connect()` again.

pytesseract does not run in python 3 without errors.  There are some small issues that need fixing to make things run without errors on python 2 and 3.

In pytesseract.py:

* All print statements need to be inside parentheses.
  ```
  print(image_to_string(image))
  ```

* StringIO needs to be imported from the io module
  ```
  from io import StringIO
  ```
* StringIO direct usage needed
  ```
  sys.stderr = StringIO()
  ```
* os.tempnam needs to be refactored because it is deprecated. https://docs.python.org/2/library/os.html#os.tempnam.    One solution is to replace:
  ```
  return os.tempnam(None, 'tess_')
  ```
  with
  ```
  import tempfile
  with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as tf:
      tmpname = 'tess_' + os.path.basename(tf.name)
  return tmpname
  ```

* file() is deprecated and should to be changed to open():
  ```
  f = open(output_file_name)
  ```

In __init__.py import needs to be explicit:

  ```
  from .pytesseract import image_to_string
  ```
  
With these changes I could run pytesseract on both python 2.7 and python 3.5.

issue_title: Enormous overhead ending with OOM on 1M data set
issue_body:
Hi! I've implemented simple empty strategy following quickstart guide. For datafeed I've loaded to memory 1M simple lines (1 min - bars for 2 years for certain ticker).

When I run this _empty_ strategy on _preloaded_ data feed on my Lenovo T410s (core i5, 8 GB RAM, Win 10 x64), memory consumption lineary increases until it throws OOM at 2 GB limit after 5-10 minutes of work!!

Either I am doing something completely wrong, or there is some big performance problem there.

Here is my code. When I uncomment "if self.__index >= 100000:" line to limit data with 100K elements, it consumes smth like 200-300MB RAM and finishes in 1 minute (for me it also seems very slow).

```
class SimpleStrategy(backtrader.Strategy):
    def __init__(self):
        # Keep a reference to the "close" line in the data[0] dataseries
        self.dataclose = self.datas[0].close

    def next(self):
        pass

class BackTraderFeed(backtrader.DataBase):
    __index = 0

    def __init__(self, bars):
        super().__init__()
        self.__bars = bars

    def _load(self):
        # if self.__index >= 100000:
        if self.__index >= len(self.__bars):
            return False
        dt, o, h, l, c, v = self.__bars[self.__index]
        self.lines.datetime[0] = dateintern.date2num(dt)
        self.lines.open[0] = o
        self.lines.high[0] = h
        self.lines.low[0] = l
        self.lines.close[0] = c
        self.lines.volume[0] = v
        self.lines.openinterest[0] = 0.0
        self.__index += 1
        return True

if __name__ == '__main__':
    cerebro = backtrader.Cerebro()
    cerebro.addstrategy(SimpleStrategy)
    _bars = # 1 M bars loaded here into memory
    data = BackTraderFeed(_bars)
    cerebro.adddata(data)
    cerebro.broker.setcash(10.0)
    logger.info('Starting Portfolio Value: %.2f', cerebro.broker.getvalue())
    cerebro.run(exactbars=1, runonce=True) # tried to experiment with perf params here, nothing helps
    logger.info('Final Portfolio Value: %.2f', cerebro.broker.getvalue())
```

IMHO an empty strategy on only 1M lines should work in several seconds (strategy has no operations - so cerebro has only to iterate) and consume smth like 1M x 100 bytes = 100 MB (alright, maybe 500 MB, but no more). My "bars" list is like 150-200mb large.

issue_title: If forking test case terminates abnormally, hypothesis will not print falsifying example
issue_body:
```python
import os

import hypothesis
from hypothesis import strategies
from hypothesis.testrunners.forking import ForkingTestCase


class TestForking(ForkingTestCase):
    @hypothesis.given(strategies.integers())
    def test_abnormal(self, x):
        os._exit(1)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    TestForking().test_abnormal()
```

outputs

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../hypothesis_forking_bug.py", line 15, in <module>
    TestForking().test_abnormal()
  File ".../.hypothesis/eval_source/hypothesis_temporary_module_....py", line 5, in test_abnormal
    return f(self, x)
  File ".../site-packages/hypothesis/core.py", line 532, in wrapped_test
    print_example=True
  File ".../site-packages/hypothesis/testrunners/forking.py", line 105, in execute_example
    raise AbnormalExit()
hypothesis.errors.AbnormalExit
```

There is no
```
Falsifying example: test_abnormal(self=<__main__.TestForking testMethod=runTest>, x=0)
```
as there would be for regular test failures.

I observed this behavior on ubuntu, cpython 3.4.3, hypothesis 1.8.0.

Could it be that composition `test_runner(reify_and_execute(..., test, ...))` in `core.py` is done in the wrong order?

`iss -v`, `iss --version` should display the version number.

could also display the version number in `--debug` mode before the programme starts.

判断 4月5日是否是节假日 
```
march_first = today + datetime.timedelta(days=1)
is_workday(march_first)  
```
_输出 FALSE_
工作日和节假日判断错误。
原因是 march_first 是  datetime 类，不是 date 类。
但是 datetime 类才有 timedelta方法，进行日期的计算。
将 march_first 转换为 date 类。
示例代码如下
```
march_first = today + datetime.timedelta(days=1)
is_workday(march_first.date())
```
如果可以的话，将上面的的示例代码进行修改。。。。
或者将 datetime 类进行强制转换为 date 类。 @LKI

Thanks!

From [change log](https://www.reqrypt.org/windivert-changelog.txt):
**WinDivert 1.4.0-rc**
    - Add a new WINDIVERT_PARAM_QUEUE_SIZE parameter that can be used to
      control the maximum number of bytes used by the packet queue.
    - Add a new WINDIVERT_FLAG_DEBUG flag that causes WinDivertSend() to
      block until the packet exits the Windows TCP/IP stack.  This is slower
      but provides better error messages, so is useful for debugging.
    - Internally queued packets are now reinjected when WinDivertClose() is
      called instead of being dropped.
    - WINDIVERT_ADDRESS now includes a Timestamp field that indicates when
      the packet was first captured by WinDivert.  The timestamp uses the
      same clock as QueryPerformanceCounter().
    - WINDIVERT_ADDRESS now includes a Loopback field that indicates whether
      the packet is a loopback packet or not.
    - The filter language has been extended to include a loopback field that
      matches loopback packets.
    - Loopback packets are now considered to be outbound-only.
    - WINDIVERT_ADDRESS now includes an Impostor field which indicates that the
      packet originated from another driver rather than from the network or
      TCP/IP stack.
    - WinDivertRecv() will eventually fail with ERROR_HOST_UNREACHABLE if
      an impostor packet enters an infinite loop (see #41).
    - The filter language has been extended to include an impostor field that 
      matches impostor packets.
    - WINDIVERT_ADDRESS now includes three new flags relating to checksums:
      * PseudoIPChecksum
      * PseudoTCPChecksum
      * PseudoUDPChecksum
      When set, these flags indicate that the corresponding packet uses
      "pseudo" IP/TCP/UDP checksums instead of the default full checksums.
      Pseudo checksums are cheaper to calculate, and can be used when the
      network hardware supports checksum offloading.
    - WinDivertHelperCalcChecksums() now takes an optional pointer to a
      WINDIVERT_ADDRESS structure, and calculates pseudo checksums if the
      corresponding Pseudo*Checksum flag is set.
    - The WINDIVERT_HELPER_NO_REPLACE flag for WinDivertHelperCalcChecksums()
      has been deprecated.  It is no longer necessary to calculate checksums
      for unmodified packets.
**WinDivert 1.4.1**
    - Dual license WinDivert under LGPLv3 and GPLv2.
**WinDivert 1.4.2**
    - Add workaround for pseudo checksum issue (see #134).
**WinDivert 1.4.3**
    - WinDivert.dll no longer depends on MSVCRT*.dll.

It's "inadvisable" to rearrange worksheets once they have been added, according to [this Stack Overflow answer](http://stackoverflow.com/a/21124112/95852). So I looked into possible reasons why, and it seems to come down to the fact that `Workbook.worksheets_objs` and `Workbook.sheetnames` are independent lists, with no API that guarantees keeping them synchronized. In particular, `Workbook._get_sheet_index` relies on `sheetnames` being unaltered except via `Workbook._add_sheet`.

Some possibilities (not mutually exclusive) that occurred to me:
* Add a public method to insert a new sheet just before a given ordinal position or sheet name.
* Convert `sheetnames` to a property with a getter that returns `[ws.name for ws in worksheets_objs]`.
* Add a data structure to `Workbook` to map sheet names to internal worksheet indices (the `index` worksheet attribute, not ordinal position), to make `Workbook._get_sheet_index` trivial or unnecessary.
* If `sheetnames` becomes a property, give it a setter that rearranges `worksheets_objs`.

Would some combination of these relatively lightweight changes be sufficient, or is the problem deeper? Your answer stated that sorting `worksheets_objs` might work for "simple" cases but not "complex" ones. At the very least, I think these changes would increase the number cases where reordering works. (And they are simple enough that I could work on them, if there is interest.)

issue_title: No warning raised when there are more than 255 data series within a chart
issue_body:
Excel doesn't recognize charts if they have more than 255 data series. This is [documented](https://support.office.com/en-us/article/Excel-specifications-and-limits-ca36e2dc-1f09-4620-b726-67c00b05040f#bmcharting) limit. Excel doesn't allow to create a chart from a data region with 256 or more columns, it prints "The Maximum number of data series per chart is 255" when a user attempt to select such region. 

One can easily hit the limit when a chart is created programmatically:
```
import xlsxwriter                                
                                                 
workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('malformed.xlsx') 
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()             
                                                 
worksheet.write(0, 0, 'name')                    
worksheet.write(1, 0, 'value')                   
                                                 
chart = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'column'})   
for i in range(1, 257):                          
    worksheet.write(0, i, 'name{}'.format(i))    
    worksheet.write(1, i, i)                     
    chart.add_series({                           
        'name': 'name{}'.format(i),              
        'values': [worksheet.name, 1, i, 1, i]   
    })                                           
                                                 
worksheet.insert_chart(2, 0, chart)              
                                                 
workbook.close()                                 
```

When I try to open `malformed.xlsx` in Excel 2016, it proposes to repair the document and drops the chart if I confirm. LibreOffice can open the document. So I think the document is valid from Office Open XML perspective. 

I'm using python 2.7.12 and XlsxWriter 0.9.4.

XlsxWriter should raise a warning or even exception (in the next major version) if Excel hard limits are hit.

Basically, Whenever I add some validation like
``` python
import pandas schema as ps
# mumble mumble
ps.Column('some_column', [ps.validation.InListValidation(["FOO", "BAR "BAZ", "nan"])]),

```
I have to add the `"nan"` to the list to avoid having null rows being assumed non valid, considering that some rows might be empty. I understand that one might want to mark null values as invalid, so I propose different interface, something like

``` python
ps.Column('some_column', [ps.validation.InListValidation(["FOO", "BAR "BAZ"]), ignore_nan=True]),
```

This parameter ` ignore_nan` can be set as `False` by default to get the current behaviour.

issue_title: Bbox error when saving null geometries
issue_body:
Surprised this hasn't been reported earlier, but saving shapefiles containing at least one null geometry results in error (though maybe related to #22?). This because the method to calculate the bbox for the entire file attempts to use coordinate information for all shapes, but null shapes don't have coordinates:

```
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\shapefile.py", line 717, in bbox
    return self.__bbox(self._shapes)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\shapefile.py", line 687, in __bbox
    px, py = list(zip(*s.points))[:2]
ValueError: need more than 0 values to unpack
```

The bbox method looks like this:

    def __bbox(self, shapes, shapeTypes=[]):
        x = []
        y = []
        for s in shapes:
            shapeType = self.shapeType
            if shapeTypes:
                shapeType = shapeTypes[shapes.index(s)]
            px, py = list(zip(*s.points))[:2]
            x.extend(px)
            y.extend(py)
        return [min(x), min(y), max(x), max(y)]

Should be fairly easy to fix, by simply skipping each shape of type NULL. The shapeTypes arg also seems not to be unused and unnecessary, and can besides be accessed directly from each shape object via `s.shapeType`. Throwing together a PR at a later point.

issue_title: In grammar control of `prefer_shift` and `prefer_shift_over_empty` strategies
issue_body:
[`prefer_shift` and `prefer_shift_over_empty` strategies]() can be defined globally during parser instantiation. They are overridden by explicit per-production rule for associativity (`left`/`right`).

GLR parser can investigate both shift and reduce action. There are situation where we want some of the `prefer_*` strategies to be applied globally but to disable it for some of the productions.

Consider this example:
```python
    grammar = """
    Program: "begin" statements=Statement* ProgramEnd EOF;
    ProgramEnd: "end" | DOT;
    Statement: "end" "transaction" | "command";
    DOT: ".";
    """
    g = Grammar.from_string(grammar, ignore_case=True)
    parser = GLRParser(g, build_tree=True, prefer_shifts=True)

    parser.parse("""
    begin
        command
        end transaction
        command
        end transaction
        command
    end
    """)
```

If we blindly use `prefer_shifts` in GLR than `statements` in `Program` rule will not be reduced when the final `end` keyword is encountered but will be shifted in anticipation of the `end transaction` statement. Actually, we need 2 tokens of lookahead to decide if the `end` token is program end or the beginning of `end transaction` statement. Thus, here we should let GLR investigate both shift (to check if it's `end transation`) and reduce (to check if it's end of program).

The idea is to have `nops` and `nopse` disambiguation rules that will disable global settings of `prefer_shifts` and `prefer_shifts_over_empty` on a production level.

> ValueError: Input for Rastrigin function must be within [-5.12, 5.12].

The initial positions are clearly within bounds. I also checked the source code, and even tried the condition that checks for the bounds. All seems to be in order. What makes it even more puzzling is that it works perfectly fine for the Ackley function! Am I not seeing something very obvious?

My wrapper is as follows:

```
import itertools
import numpy as np
import pyswarms as ps
from pyswarms.utils.functions import single_obj as fx

def run_global_best_pso(n_dims, test_func, n_inds, n_gens,\
                        initial_positions=None,\
                        c1=0.5, c2=0.3, w=0.9,\
                        random_seed=12345
        ):
        options = {'c1':c1, 'c2':c2, 'w':w}

        optimizer = ps.single.GlobalBestPSO(n_particles=n_inds, dimensions=n_dims, options=options)
        if initial_positions is not None:
                optimizer.pos = np.array(initial_positions).copy()

        stats = optimizer.optimize(test_func, iters=n_gens)
        pos_history = optimizer.get_pos_history

        return(pos_history)

if __name__ == "__main__":
        n_inds = 100
        n_gens = 10000

        initial_positions = list(itertools.repeat([2.0, 3.0, -3.0], n_inds))

        run_global_best_pso(n_dims=3, test_func=fx.rastrigin_func,\
                                n_inds=n_inds, n_gens=n_gens,\
                                initial_positions=initial_positions)
```

Update: It works when using different values for the initial positions to solve Rastrigin, e.g., [2.0, 0, -0.02] works but [2.0, 3.0, -0.02] does not.

issue_title: how to handle large mailboxes ie 10K items with attachments
issue_body:
Love the work and the examples got me started straight away however how do you handle large mailboxes?

For me I have an inbox I want to archive all emails / attachments from and I have code that looks like this:

```python
for item in mailbox.all().iterator():
    conversation_id = item.conversation_id
    folder_id = conversation_id.id
    folder_id = folder_id.replace("/", "_")
    folder = base_folder + "/" + folder_id

    if not os.path.exists(folder):
        os.makedirs(folder)

    body = item.body.encode('utf-8')
    subject = item.subject
    subject = subject.replace(" ", "_")
    subject = subject.replace("/", "_")
    subject = subject.replace(":", "_")

    local_path = os.path.join(folder, subject + ".html")
    with open(local_path, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(body)
    print('Saved attachment to', local_path)

    for attachment in item.attachments:

        if isinstance(attachment, FileAttachment):

            local_path = os.path.join(folder, attachment.name)
            with open(local_path, 'wb') as f:
                f.write(attachment.content)
            print('Saved attachment to', local_path)
        elif isinstance(attachment, ItemAttachment):

            if isinstance(attachment.item, Message):

                body = attachment.item.body.encode('utf-8')
                subject = attachment.item.subject
                subject = subject.replace(" ", "_")
                subject = subject.replace("/", "_")
                subject = subject.replace(":", "_")

                local_path = os.path.join(folder, subject + ".html")
                with open(local_path, 'wb') as f:
                    f.write(body)
                print('Saved attachment to', local_path)


                for attachment in attachment.item.attachments:
                    if isinstance(attachment, FileAttachment):
                        # pass
                        local_path = os.path.join(folder, attachment.name)
                        with open(local_path, 'wb') as f:
                            f.write(attachment.content)
                        print('Saved attachment to', local_path)

```
I am testing this against a dummy folder on our exchange server but im watching the memory consumption and the run time as all objects are created from the all method which I can see wont work when I want to run this code against a mailbox with 10K emails.

I thought using your iterator method would do the trick based on your notes.. but im not seeing any change?

how do I do this in a more optimised manner ?

Thankyou

...
# global configuration

issue_title: Variable size of vertices and triangles per frame
issue_body:
I already slightly modified the internals in a local repo to accommodate for the variable sized triangle animation but now I would like to also have variable sized vertices over time.

I know this is an issue with the interpolation in between the frames and that both previous and the next frames vertex attributes need to be stored and sent for rendering in the shaders which can interpolate between the vertex values. So if the size would be variable that interpolation wouldn't be possible. 

But I don't really see the benefit of the interpolation of vertex attributes between frames because if it comes to looking at your simulation results for example you only want to see what is generated by the simulation and not what is interpolated in between the frames by the visualization. Maybe for aesthetic purposes it is nice to interpolate but otherwise it only gives a false representation of your results in between the frames.

Now my idea was to make a dynamic mesh widget but before I start I wanna know if that wasn't already in the pipeline for you? What do you think about dynamic amount of vertices per frame?

issue_title: Using get_user() in the UserDatastore instead of find_user()
issue_body:
## Issue:
I am extending the login/register routine and customizing the UserDatastore using username in addition to email (although this question would apply more to those not using an email) with custom Groups and other Items.  When automating role creation via signals (user_registered or user_confirmed) and passing the user to [add_role_to_user(self, user, role)](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L99) it passes to [_prepare_role_modify_args(self, user, role)](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L70) which in turn uses the [find_user](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L72) method which looks up only for the email for that user and returns none if something else is passed to it.  In my case I was passing the username, which causes it to find none. 

As mentioned I am extending the current UserDatastore and using similar methods for adding Groups and Projects/Items which have similar relationships to the User Model and thus using similar methods to do so.  I am using the [get_user()](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/datastore.py#L87) method to capture my User Model which looks for the [indentity](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/utils.py#L377) as set in [configuration variable](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/core.py#L92) which returns the user model the same as the find_user() method.

## Question: 
Is there an advantage to using find_user() over get_user() in the role lookup?  It would seem using the get_user() method you would make it a bit easier to customize and would solve some of the issues with custom implementations that I have seen here and provide for more flexibility for the developer and extensibility in the future.

If get_user() was used instead of find_user() would it break something somewhere else or cause some compatibility issues that I am not seeing?  It would appear that get_user() is extended to the Peewee and MongoDB Classes and appear to return exactly the same as the SQL Class. 

This caused me a little bit of a headache figuring out, however I am able to use it as is with my current setup.  If you were not using an email in your model it would not be possible to use this method to add a user to a role and would require you to bypass large portions of the script to make your customization work.


I am happy make the changes and submit a PR if there are no objections.

I am having some issues with certain browsers (mainly on embedded devices) that send ping (opcode 0x09) and/or pong (opcode 0x0A) frames without being solicited.

With the current implementation, those frames are handled like any text message (opcode 0x01) and there is no way to differenciate them from actual messages. The only workaround I found is to check if the payload of the frame matches a specific text (e.g. "PING"). IMHO the solution is rather unclean. 

I am thinking of two ways to deal with the issue:
- either make the server automatically send a pong frame whenever it receives a ping frame and not call the callback message_received()
- add another callback which is called when a ping frame is received and let the user deal with it.

I can probably help with the code if needed.

Thank you by advance for reading :)

while it's nice that you can change the delimiter from space to whatever string, it would be even better if xkcdpass would have the (optionnal) feature to inject random symbols and digits as separators.

by default, the search space of xkcdpass-generated passwords is quite large, but that's mostly due to the length of the password. while one can't assume that the password to be cracked will be all lowercase letters in the general case, that means the password generation routine becomes yet another "something you know" and allows the attacker to reduce the search space significantly once they know you use xkcdpass.

allowing for random passwords would force attackers to not limit themselves to 26 characters. adding numbers and symbols to that space would add about 40 bits of entropy to every password, at little cost to the human memory.

```
[1020]anarcat@curie:~$ xkcdpass  | wc
      1       6      46
[1021]anarcat@curie:~$ xkcdpass  | wc
      1       6      46

> log2((26+20)^55)

  log2((26 + 20)^55) = approx. 303.79591

> log2((26+20)^46)

  log2((26 + 20)^46) = approx. 254.08385

> log2((26)^46)

  log2(26^46) = approx. 216.22023

> 
```

thanks for your consideration!

issue_title: How to configure the mode option of a CodeMirror widget for C++?
issue_body:
As the [language modes](http://codemirror.net/mode/index.html) page shows, for the C++ language CodeMirror only has a C-like mode, and it seems that to make a proper configuration for C++, we have to
1. set the name of the mode to 'clike' to load the appropriate mode file (`clike.js` in this case), and
2. set the MIME type to 'text/x-c++src', as suggested at the bottom of [this page](http://codemirror.net/mode/clike/index.html), since the clike mode is not C++-specific.

And how can I achieve that?

For reference, the CodeMirror [manual](http://codemirror.net/doc/manual.html#config) says
>mode: string|object
The mode to use.  It may be a string, which either simply names the mode or is a MIME type associated with the mode. Alternatively, it may be an object containing configuration options for the mode, with a name property that names the mode (for example {name: "javascript", json: true})

issue_title: Support serializer "hints" to use customized fields classes
issue_body:
DRF ModelSerializer has such hints as a class attributes:
```
    serializer_related_field = PrimaryKeyRelatedField
    serializer_related_to_field = SlugRelatedField
    serializer_url_field = HyperlinkedIdentityField
    serializer_choice_field = ChoiceField
```

DocumentSerializer uses hardcoded classes:
* `ReferenceField` for 'relations', in 
* `GenericReferenceField` for 'relations' w/out related_model
* `GenericEmbeddedDocumentField` for generic embeddings

This should be moved into hints the same way as in DRF. 

And classes to autocreate nested serializers should also be hintable (unike DRF), to allow recursive customized serializers.

You can see this problem if you copy the following code into a controller:

def initTestTable():
    db.testTable.insert(testFloat=0.1)
    tuples=db(db.testTable.id>0).select(db.testTable.ALL)
    return locals()

def updateTestTable():
    db(db.testTable.id==1).update(testFloat=db.testTable.testFloat+1.0) # EITHER NOT WORKING AT ALL OR IS TRYING TO CONCATINATE
    print db._lastsql

    db(db.testTable.id==1).update(testFloat=db.testTable.testFloat-(-1.0)) # THIS IS WORKING
    print db._lastsql
    
    tuples=db(db.testTable.id>0).select(db.testTable.ALL)
    return locals()

Hi!

I'm using pydal in an app backend with about 50 table definitions (`define_table`). The DAL object is created for each HTTP request, and discarded after the request ends.

The DAL(...) initialization time takes between 30-40ms (with disabled migrations), which is very long to construct the static database definition. This limits the max req/s to ~25. The db schema also doesn't change between requests, so I think it's wasted time. There are also a lot of checks and assertions in the `define_table()` and `Table()` constructor, which must not be executed for each request.

What I was first thinking of is to create the table definitions only the first time (during app startup), and to reuse the table definitions for all requests. This is somehow difficult, because each Table object has a reference to the DAL object. I would have to turn all references to the new created DAL object. No idea if this can work at all.

Instead I tried to not destroy the DAL objects but to store it in a queue after the request, and reuse it for the next request:

```python
queue = gevent.queue.Queue()

def request():
    if not queue.qsize():
        queue.put_nowait(create_dal(db_uri))

    db = queue.get_nowait()
    db._adapter.reconnect()

    try:
        # handle the request
        handle_request(db)
        db.commit()
        queue.put(db)
    except:
        db.rollback()
```

The `db._adapter.reconnect()` assigns the current thread (or greenlet) a connection, and tests if the db connection is still alive. If the request raises an error, I do not reuse the DAL object.

The first PoC in my app backend shows that this pattern seems to work, having a major performance boost. Does anybody know if the reuse of the DAL objects can have any side effects I am currently not aware of?

issue_title: test_loss.test_ctc_loss_train has fixed seed that can mask flakiness
issue_body:
The unit test in title have been using fixed seed to mask flakiness. Suggested action:
1. Evaluate whether the test is flaky without fixed seed. If not, remove seed. Else move to 2
2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

issue_title: predict(model, val) produces fewer number of samples compared to original sample size
issue_body:
I have noticed a puzzling phenomenon. Every time I predict on a rec file, the number of samples becomes fewer and fewer. 
Let's say train and val are the rec files generated by im2rec.py. Suppose the model is trained as follows.
model <- mx.model.FeedForward.create(
  symbol             = new_soft,
  X                  = train,
  #eval.data          = val, I removed this part as it's optional and will use val for validation outside. 
  ctx                = mx.gpu(0),
  eval.metric        = mx.metric.accuracy,
  num.round          = 1,
  learning.rate      = 0.05,
  momentum           = 0.9,
  wd                 = 0.00001,
  kvstore            = "local",
  array.batch.size   = 128,
  epoch.end.callback = mx.callback.save.checkpoint("my_model"),
  batch.end.callback = mx.callback.log.train.metric(150),
  initializer        = mx.init.Xavier(factor_type = "in", magnitude = 2.34),
  optimizer          = "sgd",
  arg.params         = arg_params_new,
  aux.params         = inception_bn$aux.params
)
probs = predict(model, val)
The first time I executed this command, ncol(probs) is the correct number of validation size. 
The second time I execute the command, ncol(probs) has 98 less number of samples compared to the correct validation size.
The third time I execute the command, ncol(probs) has 189 less number of samples compared to the correct validation size. 
I believe ncol(probs)'s size should be the same as the validation size no mater how many times I run the predict command. Can you help explain what went wrong here or what I can do to ensure I get the correct number of samples back. Thanks!

I find that names like HybridBlock, HybridSequential, and hybridize() in gluon are not effectively conveying what their utilities and usages are. To me, 'HybridBlock' sounds like a mixture of 'Block' and something else, although it is actually an extension of a Block. Also, calling hybridize() sounds like imperative and symbolic program will be mixed in executions, although in reality, calling it makes an one-way transition of the block from an imperative program to a symbolic program. Lastly, 'hybrid' is an ambiguous word which meaning is unclear until a user takes a careful look at the hybrid computation tutorial.

Proposal: Why don't we call these CompilableBlock, CompilableSequential, and compile()? Then, it should be clear CompilableBlocks are extensions of Blocks, and executions of CompilableBlocks will be faster after calling compile() than they were before, because 'compile' is a concept most programmers are familiar with.

Furthermore, from the API design point of view, it might be clearer if compile() creates a new Block rather than changing the state of an exiting HybridBlock. Then it should be clear that CompilableBlock is still an imperative program, whereas CompilableBlock.compile() is a declarative program.

I am curious what others' thoughts on this are. Tagging @szha since I talked with him on this separately.

How to make step-by-step predictions using RNN with bucketing
issue_body:
By ‘step-by-step predictions’ I mean using values sampled from current time step's output as input in the next time step. For example, to generate a sentence using the lstm model in example/rnn/lstm_bucketing.py, I need to start from a particular word, feed it to the network and get outputs, then sample a word from the outputs and fix it as the input in the next time step.    

Now in every time step I need to construct a data batch and pad the sequence to a legal length(one of the buckets' length) because forward() can only accept DataBatch.  Are there any existing interfaces (I found a LSTMInferenceModel in tutorial but it seems to be deprecated.) or methods to make it a more elegant process?

Thanks!

issue_title: Issues with scheduler (using celery) after upgrade to 1.6.1
issue_body:
Hi,

First, many thanks for developing and open sourcing Airflow. We have been using it for the past few months and it has greatly improved our workflow process.

After upgrading to 1.6.1, we've had a few troubles with the scheduler.
1. Pools that existed prior to the upgrade will not run jobs anymore. We created new pools with different names and assigned our dags to them.
2. Sometimes, though a DAG run will start (can see this in the UI and in the scheduler logs), the tasks for that DAG run will not be queued.
3. Sometimes there is a long delay between the time when a task gets queued and when it gets worked on, even if there are available slot in the pool.

I was doing some research and noticed that here (https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/airbnb_airflow/Bf-7YASKD2U) and in #698 it was recommended to run the scheduler for 5 runs at a time before restarting it. This seems to have helped some.

For the third issue, I noticed a 20 minute delay between seeing this in the scheduler logs:
2015-12-10 17:27:38,852 - root - INFO - Adding to queue: airflow run Airflow-Scheduler-Monitor heartbeat 2015-12-10T16:31:00   --local     -sd DAGS_FOLDER/scheduler-monitor.py  

And seeing this in the worker log:
Logging into: /r9/airflow/logs/Airflow-Scheduler-Monitor/heartbeat/2015-12-10T16:31:00

Is there a good way to look at whats happening between these two log messages, either with more logging or connecting to look at the celery queues?

Thanks,
Stephen

issue_title: Feed CTC algorithm with inputs that are log(probabilities)
issue_body:
The test[[1](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/python/kernel_tests/ctc_decoder_ops_test.py#L96)] of `tf.nn.ctc_greedy_decoder()` implies that its `inputs` parameter  is expected to be the log base _e_ of probabilities exported from the network.

Furthermore, the implementation[[2](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/kernels/ctc_decoder_ops.cc#L177)] of `tf.nn.ctc_greedy_decoder()` outputs not log probabilities, as documented, but negative log probabilities[[3](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/kernels/ctc_decoder_ops.cc#L221)].

With this in mind, and the assumption that the beam search decoder expects inputs of the same form and gives outputs of the same form, the current code should be switched to feed the decoder log base _e_ of the probabilities exported from the network.

A question to answer is if the `ctc_loss()` function can also be passed `inputs` that is the `np.log()` log base _e_ of probabilities exported from the network.

If this question is answered affirmatively, then the `BiRNN()` should output log base _e_ probabilities. If it is answered negatively, then `BiRNN()` should stay as is and the input to the decoder should be properly modified.

issue_title: Add ability to convert entire request body into an object type
issue_body:
e.g.

***Request Body***

```json
{
  "foo": "bar",
  "item": 1
}
```

***Logic Code***

```python
from doctor.types import integer, Object, string

class Req(Object):
    description = 'A request body'
    properties = {
        'foo': string('A string'),
        'item': integer('A number'),
    }
    required = ['foo']

def my_func(req: Req):
    print(req.foo)  # outputs 'bar'


create_routes((
    Route('/foo/', methods=[
        post(my_func, req_obj=Req)],
))
```

In essence it allows converting an entire request body into a single object.

issue_title: Incorrect comparison of original/retrofitted taxonomies in the BCR calculator
issue_body:
For the BCR calculator, [the check](https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/blob/engine-2.7/openquake/risklib/riskinput.py#L110-L111) comparing taxonomies from the original and retrofitted vulnerabilities (see snippet below) may fail because python doesn't iterate through the dictionary keys in sorted order, even when both vulnerability models have exactly the same imts and taxonomies.

```python
# classical_bcr calculator
for (taxonomy, vf_orig), (taxonomy_, vf_retro) in \
        zip(rmdict.items(), retrodict.items()):
    assert taxonomy == taxonomy_  # same imt and taxonomy
    self._riskmodels[taxonomy] = riskmodels.get_riskmodel(
        taxonomy, oqparam,
        vulnerability_functions_orig=vf_orig,
        vulnerability_functions_retro=vf_retro)
```

It should be replaced with SQLite. Probably it is best to remove the table job_stats and merge it with the table oq_job. Also the log table can probably be refactored. Moreover we can merge the authentication database in the main database. Finally we should merge `openquake.engine.settings` inside `openquake.server.settings`.

issue_title: Finding alternate object directories on open
issue_body:
Git 2.11 introduced a change such that incoming objects in a push are sequestered in an "alternate object directory." It appears that libgit2 supports opening repositories with these directories, taking certain environment variables into consideration, but the latest pygit2 does not invoke the appropriate function (`git_repository_open_ext()` from what I can tell). Am I correct in this assessment? Is there a way to work around this with the current code, or is a patch necessary?

If that didn't make sense, let me explain at a higher level: I'm writing a receive hook in Python and using pygit2 to examine the incoming commits. With versions of Git prior to 2.11 this is not a problem since the incoming objects are stored directly in the odb. However, now they are located elsewhere and pygit2 does not see them.

The partial_ratio calculation seems to yield incorrect results for certain combinations of strings when it uses the python-Levenshtein SequenceMatcher.

This works well:
```python
> fuzz.partial_ratio('this is a test', 'is this is a not this is a test!')
> 100
```

But changing the string slightly, while not affecting the target

```python
> fuzz.partial_ratio('this is a test', 'is this is a not really thing this is a test!') 
> 92
```

Digging deeper, it appears that the get_matching_blocks() method returns substrings that do not actually match the string we are searching for, so the subsequent ratio calculations are performed on a set of poorly-matched ones. 

Removing python-Levenshtein and using the python-only SequenceMatcher makes that method perform its job correctly. I couldn't figure out what it was about certain strings that made it break, after trying a whole bunch.

To top it off, the python-Levenshtein library appears to have been left unsupported for a while now. Any ideas? Maybe for now, removing the recommendation to use python-Levenshtein would let code run correctly, if not as fast? Thanks!

issue_title: add support for a watchdog-based file monitor if available
issue_body:
I do not want `hupper` to depend on `watchdog` but if it's available we should support it. The reason is that watchdog has a few tricky and unnecessary dependencies and seems to be not very actively maintained right now so a hard dependency would make hupper unavailable on certain systems.

This basically means writing an alternate to the `PollingFileMonitor` and having `start_reloader` attempt to use it if watchdog is available. Finally we could add an extra requirement such as `hupper[watchdog]` which depends on watchdog and update the documents as such.

issue_title: Use booleans arguments like in Robot Framework
issue_body:
RF convenience methods for handling booleans, example 
```
| Some Keyword | False |
```
Is evaluated as False in Robot Framework standard libraries, although the argument is string and according Python it should evaluate as True. Changing the S2L to behave in similar fashion as the Robot Framework standard libraries could be useful.

More details in http://robotframework.org/robotframework/latest/libraries/BuiltIn.html#Boolean%20arguments and some discussion in #717

issue_title: Cannot switch windows on browsers which don't support javascript
issue_body:
When using a web browser that doesn't support javascript  (such as [QtWebDriver](https://github.com/cisco-open-source/qtwebdriver)) using the `Select Window` keyword results in an error:
```
WebDriverException: Message: Script execution failed. Script: return [ window.id, window.name, document.title, document.URL ];;
 Current view doesnt support this command.
```
even though this operation can be done through the web driver API.

Selenium2Library and QtWebDriver are checked out from their respective git development branches, Robot Framework version 3.0.2, Selenium version 3.3.1.

Pull request #788 fixes this issue.

issue_title: Updated tileset name not found in layer, but original name is.
issue_body:
This is very strange and may be a server side issue. Unsure of where else to post this.

I have an application where several times a day, I need to upload updated geojson data. I'd really like to be able to reuse the tileset name but overwrite the old data entirely (since I can't delete except in map box studio, which would be very cumbersome). If I upload data with the same name (or no name), the old timestamps remain which is a problem for querying the dataset.  If I reuse the tileset but supply a new name, the old timestamps are gone and the studio tile shows the new name. However, my map then gives me "Error: Source layer "test2" does not exist on source "hsig_dir" as specified by style layer "dir" — style.js:103". Surprisingly, if I supply the first name "test1" as a source-layer, my map appears to work. If I use the web "replace tileset" option and give it a different filename, the name is updated and all is good. However, this needs to be automated and work in python.

I suspect somewhere, there are some data tags that aren't being updated.  "service.list().json()" shows that the name = u'test3' on the upload which makes me think it's on the server.

The same behavior occurs with the CLI client supplying --name test2. 

Any help would be appreciated. I've been stalled on this issue for quite a while.

issue_title: Are the mecanum wheel functions actually for mecanum drive trains?
issue_body:
I know this question seems strange but the documentation describes a mecanum drive train as such:
```
A method for driving with Mecanum wheeled robots. There are 4 wheels
        on the robot, arranged so that the front and back wheels are toed in
        45 degrees.  When looking at the wheels from the top, the roller
        axles should form an X across the robot.
```

This doesn't seem to be the case with mecanum wheels. As seen in any demonstration or diagram they are not toed in at 45 degree angles. They re placed like regular wheels. The ones which ARE toed in at 45 degree angles are the omni wheels. 
Mecanum: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q4esxUwpzWQ
Omni: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5vJCucpVdX0

I was just about to test the code on our mecanum drive train then I noticed what it says in the docs. So I am not sure if this will actually work for mecanum or not. I may need to just convert the mecanum code on the FRC site.

just adding this on `~/.bash_profile` is enough to fully utilize pipenv features?
