issue_title: Unknown result in checking a first-order formula from the real closed field.
issue_body:
I am sorry if this was already discussed but I am new as a Z3 user. I am using Z3 to theorem prove first-order formulae stating properties of the real numbers. I am not doing anything in particular except declaring the formula in which every symbol used is declared as Real. The first-order theory of the real numbers is decidable as it is isomorphic to the real closed field [Tarski 1951] for which many decision procedures have been proposed, at least two with double exponential elementary complexity [Monk 1974, Collins 1975] and one with exponential space or parallel exponential time elementary complexity [Ben-Or 1986]. Based on this, I do not understand why Z3 responds Unknown when it could take a lot, but answer Sat or Unsat. 

Best regards and thanks.

For a following query:

```
(declare-fun __POLICY_CHOICE_3 () Int)
(declare-fun __INITIAL_LOCATION () Int)
(declare-fun |main::k@2| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::k@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::n@3| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::j@2| () Int)
(declare-fun |__VERIFIER_assert::cond@2| () Int)
(declare-fun |main::j@3| () Int)
(assert (let ((a!1 (or (and (not (<= |__VERIFIER_assert::cond@2| 0))
                    (= __POLICY_CHOICE_3 0))
               (and (not (>= |__VERIFIER_assert::cond@2| 0))
                    (= __POLICY_CHOICE_3 1)))))
  (and (= __INITIAL_LOCATION 1)
       (not (<= |main::n@3| |main::j@2|))
       (= |__VERIFIER_assert::cond@2| (ite (<= |main::k@2| 0) 0 1))
       a!1
       (= |main::j@3| (+ 1 |main::j@2|))
       (= |main::k@3| (+ (- 1) |main::k@2|)))))
(assert (let ((a!1 (<= (+ (+ |main::j@2| (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) |main::n@3|) 0))
      (a!2 (<= (+ (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) |main::k@2|) |main::n@3|) 2))
      (a!3 (<= (+ (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) |main::n@3|)
               0)))
  (and (= __INITIAL_LOCATION 1)
       true
       (<= (+ |main::j@2| |main::n@3|) 1)
       (<= (+ |main::k@2| |main::n@3|) 2)
       (<= (+ |main::j@2| |main::k@2|) 1)
       (<= |main::n@3| 1)
       (<= (+ (+ |main::j@2| |main::k@2|) |main::n@3|) 2)
       a!1
       a!2
       (<= |main::j@2| 0)
       (<= |main::k@2| 1)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) |main::k@2|) 1)
       a!3
       (<= (+ |main::j@2| (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::k@2|) 0)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) 0)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) |main::n@3|) 1)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::k@2|) |main::n@3|) 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) 0))))
(assert true)
(maximize (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@3|) (* (- 1) |main::k@3|) |main::n@3|))
(set-option :opt.optsmt_engine basic)
(set-option :opt.priority box)
(check-sat)
```

I get the bound "oo".

It is obviously incorrect by looking at the query, but it's even more obvious if we ask Z3 itself whether 
`(= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@3|) (* (- 1) |main::k@3|) |main::n@3|) 10)` (or any number greater than `0` in general) is satisfiable, because the bound should be indeed zero.

The slice of the query essentially resolves to "maximize -j'-k'+n' s.t. k' = k -1 /\ j'=j+1 /\ n'=n /\ -j-k+n<=0 ", which does not change the bound.

`add_git_dir_dependency()` was updated as per issue #1227 to handle git worktrees.  Unfortunately, it fails if z3 is being used as subrepository, failing with the following:

    -- Found git worktree
    CMake Error at cmake/git_utils.cmake:37 (message):
      Found git worktree dir but could not find
      "../../.git/modules/repos/z3/commondir"


I suggest that instead of checking if .git is a file and trying to follow it via cmake, you instead use `git rev-parse --git-common-dir` and `git rev-parse --git-dir` instead.   This former invocation will get you the base .git dir, and the latter will get you the directory with the HEAD file.  This should work with all .git repositories: worktrees, subrepos, and normal.

issue_title: Enum34 is not compatible with Python 3.6
issue_body:
We've been hitting a bug with PyFilesystem2 in conjunction with Python 3.6. Depending on how the Python path is set up we are unable to use the enum features introduced in 3.6 if we also install PyFilesystem2.

The problem is that [enum34 does not support Python 3.6](https://bitbucket.org/stoneleaf/enum34/issues/19/enum34-isnt-compatible-with-python-36) and should therefore not be installed if the version used is greater than Python 3.4.

This is what you can use to test it locally:

```bash
virtualenv -p python3.6 venv
venv/bin/pip install fs
PYTHONPATH='./venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages' venv/bin/python -c "from enum import IntFlag"
```

Can this be changed? An option might be to use [enum-compat](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/enum-compat) which installs enum34 conditionally. I see there's also some sort of version-specific dependency in your setup.py though I haven't ever encountered that before.

@papajohn @SamLau95 
Hi! I'm the GSI for the smart cities connector course. I talked to Sam about the mapping tool this morning. I was wondering if we could add a method to overlay map features over an existing map. Basically the issue I ran into is that after I create a map I may want to add markers or labels to the map, and so far there's not an easy way to do that:

I wrote the following method that seems to do the trick. Could this be added to the Map tool?
The only other issue with the function is that in my implementation I edit the Map._folium_map directly. It might be nice if we copy and return a new Map object instead of modifying the existing Map in place. I couldn't figure out the best way to do this. If you have time, it would be great if you could take a look. Let me know if you have any questions.

Best,
Maddie

def overlay(feature, ds_map, color='Blue', opacity=.6):
    '''
    feature can be a table of map features, a list of map features,
    a Map, or a Region. The features will be drawn on the existing 
    Map (ds_map). 
    '''
        
    if type(feature) == Table:
        # if table of features e.g. Table.from_records(taz_map.features)
        if 'feature' in feature:
            feature = feature['feature']
            
        # if marker table e.g. table with columns: latitudes,longitudes,popup,color,radius
        else:
            feature = Circle.map_table(feature)
            
    if type(feature) in [list, np.ndarray]:
        for f in feature:
            f._attrs['fill_color'] = color
            f._attrs['fill_opacity'] = opacity
            f.draw_on(ds_map._folium_map)
    
    elif type(feature) == Map:
        for i in range(len(feature._features)):
            f = feature._features[i]
            f._attrs['fill_color'] = color
            f._attrs['fill_opacity'] = opacity
            f.draw_on(ds_map._folium_map)
    elif type(feature) == Region:
        feature._attrs['fill_color'] = color
        feature._attrs['fill_opacity'] = opacity
        feature.draw_on(ds_map._folium_map)
    return ds_map

issue_title: remove manual hoisting when updating to the next lerna release
issue_body:
in #1420, we worked around a known bug in lerna 2.0.0-beta.36 by manually removing the `immutable` library from `packages/commutable/node_modules/immutable` (because it is problematic for flow.

When we update to the next release of lerna, we should add back the `--hoist immutable` flag to our lerna command in `package.json` and remove the `rimraf /packages.../immutable` portion.

I've run into this a few times before, and i've found it again now. Basically, poking at this AceBear chal with angr. I execute till right past the read of username, then load that memory pointer. The pointer that angr returns is wrong endian. Interestingly enough, if I manually reverse the endianness and load that memory area, i get the expected input. However, this is causing the program to also read the wrong memory area and go off the tracks.

This was started through a script that first set stdin input to start with a bunch of "A" values. Expected output would be that the read would have read in that bunch of "A" values. Yes, i seeked back to 0 :-p
```
In [1]: simgr.explore(find=0x08048841)
Out[1]: <SimulationManager with 1 found>

In [3]: s = simgr.found[0].copy()

In [4]: s
Out[4]: <SimState @ 0x8048841>

# This is wrong endian. Both to me, and to angr internals itself.
In [7]: s.memory.load(s.regs.ebp + 8)
Out[7]: <BV32 0x78fefe7f>

# Note here, the specified input ("AAA..." doesn't show up)
In [8]: s.memory.load(s.memory.load(s.regs.ebp + 8))
Out[8]: <BV32 Reverse(mem_78fefe7f_3_32)>

In [12]: s.se.any_str(s.memory.load(s.regs.ebp + 8))
Deprecation warning: Use eval(expr, cast_to=str) instead of any_str
Out[12]: 'x\xfe\xfe\x7f'

In [15]: s.posix.dumps(0)
Out[15]: 'AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA\n2dfcfffbfffffffEfffffffffffffff4fffffffffff3ffffffffffffffffffff\n'

# Ask angr to tell me for real what the value is..
In [17]: hex(s.se.any_int(s.memory.load(s.regs.ebp + 8)))
Deprecation warning: Use eval() instead of any_int
Out[17]: '0x78fefe7f'

# I manually reverse endianness, load memory and find my expected "A" values.
In [19]: s.memory.load(0x7ffefe78)
Out[19]: <BV32 0x41414141>
```

I believe the last time I reported this, the claim was that internal to angr it reversed things correctly. In this binary it certainly does not. It ends up splitting and deadending with what is actually supposed to be fully concrete data.

#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```python
import numpy as np
from pyspectral.rayleigh import Rayleigh
from pyspectral.utils import debug_on
debug_on()

msi = Rayleigh('Sentinel-2C', 'msi')

sunz = np.array([[32., 40.], [31., 41.]])
satz = np.array([[45., 20.], [46., 21.]])
ssadiff = np.array([[110, 170], [120, 180]])

refl_cor_red = msi.get_reflectance(sunz, satz, ssadiff, 'B02')

```

#### Problem description

In the case that a new satellite not yet supported with the spectral responses in PySpectral, a download will  be attempted when a band name is provided to the `get_reflectance` method of the `Rayleigh` object. 

To avoid trying to download RSR data in vain (cases where even after download the rsr data for the requested sensor and platform will anyway not be there) one needs to add a check if the downloaded RSR data are the latest. This kind of checking is already implemented for the atm correction LUTs.


#### Expected Output
So, currently with the code example above a download is attempted, even if RSR data are the most recent. As seen in the `IOError` below it attempts to download the data since there is no file for S2C. But, if you repeat the run it will do the same again. 

Instead, it should detect that the latest RSR data have been downloaded and are available, and if the S2C RSR data are not part of the data, it can throw an `IOError` as is done.

issue_title: kinto CLI command to move records from a collection to another while keeping their last_modified value.
issue_body:
The thing is to make sure to create all the record from the lower `last_modified` record to the greatest one so that we keep the exact same `last_modified` value for the records.

The syntax could be:

```
   kinto move-collection-records /buckets/fennec/collections/experiments/records /buckets/fennec-staging/collections/experiments/records
```

- [ ] All the records should be created keeping their original data
- [ ] An option --with-permissions can be set to also copy the records with their original permissions.

issue_title: second PCA analysis on the same TrajectoryIterator leads to error
issue_body:
suppose you have a `TrajectoryIterator` instance `traj` and you apply pca analysis with `fit=True`:

```
import pytraj as pt
pcadata1 =  pt.pca(traj, mask=':1-10@CA', fit=True, ref=0)  
# then run the second pca analysis on the same traj (e.g you perhaps want to apply different masks/ref)
pcadata2 =  pt.pca(traj, mask=':11-20@CA', fit=True, ref=1)  
```
It will give you error:
```
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'box'
```

I locate the error to the function `TrajectoryCpptraj::_remove_transformations` in file: `./pytraj/trajectory/c_traj/c_trajectory.pyx`. The `self._actionlist` is initialized without correctly specifying topology:
```
self._actionlist = ActionList()
```
should be 
```
self._actionlist = ActionList(top=self.top)
```

Actually, I worry that even after the fix, the code will still leak memory. So it is perhaps better to replace the first two initialization lines 
```
self._actionlist = ActionList()
self._cdslist = CpptrajDatasetList()
```
with 
```
self._initialize_actionlist()
```
So that memory is properly freed after each pca analysis.

See my branch of the code for the fix proposal.

should

- [x] may be turn on `openmp` for pytraj as default.

- [x] check cpptraj's `config.h` if `openmp` was used

- [x]  may be try linking to cpptraj with openmp flag to check if cpptraj was installed with openmp? try `nm` command?

- [x]  should check cpptraj version in Version.h to avoid wasting compiling cython files if version is too old.

- [x] if turning on openmp as default for pytraj, need to update Makefile in Ambertools/src/ too,

issue_title: BUG: erroneous detail coefficients returned by downcoef with level > 1
issue_body:
As pointed out by @jairongfu in #72, it seems that there is still a problem for ``downcoef`` with detail coefficients and level > 1.  Unfortunately, the tests i added in #72 only tested the approximation case!

example reproducing the problem:
```python
import pywt
import numpy as np
from numpy.testing import assert_array_almost_equal
r = np.random.randn(16)
a2, d2, d1 = pywt.wavedec(r, 'haar', level=2)

assert_array_almost_equal(a2, pywt.downcoef('a', r, wavelet='haar', level=2))
assert_array_almost_equal(d2, pywt.downcoef('d', r, wavelet='haar', level=2))
```
The second ``assert_array_almost_equal`` call above fails, but the first passes.

What do you think about renaming this project to `scrapy-splash` to follow the standard naming convention of [scrapy plugins](https://github.com/scrapy-plugins)?

In the past, we had another `scrapy-splash` repository (that could conflict with this) but that is now gone and removed for good.

The present idea is to use different tables for each environment. This seems to be working, but I am wondering if there is a more recommended approach. 
Thanks for this awesome utility!

issue_title: Using HW Sync on D400 series with photoswitch to sync single shot capture
issue_body:
I have a D415 camera which I would like to sync a single frame capture with a photoelectric switch triggering (on a conveyor, tripped by an object).  How can I configure my D415 to listen on the HW sync pin header for the signal and to NOT take any frames (such that wait_for_frame() will not return with any data) until the signal indicates an object has arrived?

I want my image capture thread to block until a single frame is received, and that the frame exposure time is constant (with auto exposure turned off) so that I get very repeatable depth image captures of these objects on a conveyor.. 

I understand that there is new firmware available to enable the HW sync functionality on the cameras? my cameras are from the first public batch released in January 2018. 

A secondary question to this is - is there a firmware update tool which runs on Linux? I looked recently (month ago?) and saw only windows based firmware update tools. I have cameras in embedded enclosures and it's not going to be super easy to get the cable out and plug into a windows laptop to perform a firmware update. The camera is normally connected to an embedded linux micro PC.

### Issue Description
Hi,

My D415 camera is attached to a manipulator arm robot, so I had to use a 5 meter cable to connect the camera to my PC. I'm using two USB 3.0 cables of 3m and 2m (in addition to the the small cable that came with the camera).

Basically, sometimes the camera works and sometimes it stops communicating. Every several minutes I need to unplug the cable and plug it back to fix the communication.

When I use realsense-viewer, after some time I get these error messages:

- Incomplete frame arrived
- xioctl(VIDIOC_DQBUF) failed last error
- USB REC overflow
- USB SCP overflow
- IR stream start failure
- Depth stream start failure

I work with Python, and I tried to reduce the size of the data from the camera by disabling the streams that I'm not using (I only use the depth stream in the highest resolution) and increasing the waiting time for a new frame to arrive. Here's the code:
```
    config = rs.config()
    config.disable_all_streams()
    config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 1280, 720, rs.format.z16, 30)
    config.enable_stream(rs.stream.infrared, 1280, 720, rs.format.y8, 30)
    pipeline = rs.pipeline()
    pipeline.start(config)   
    waiting_for_frame = True
    timeout_ms = 5000
    while waiting_for_frame:
        try:
            frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames(timeout_ms)
            waiting_for_frame = False
        except:
            pipeline.stop()
            timeout_ms += 1000
            time.sleep(1)
            pipeline.start(config)
```
What I did may improved the situation, but didn't solve the problem.

I know that using a 5 meter cable is not in the camera's specs, but this is a constraint that I have to work with.

Does anyone have a better solution for making the camera send depth images without crashing every several minutes?

Thanks

Currently OT supports Mesh Header with Hops Left field set to 14 or less. However this is not always true, and we need to support Deep Hops Left field. 

Also the algorithm of choosing Hops Left is described in 5.10.1.1 however it is also a little implementation dependent.

I think this field may be set to 14 if calculated (predicted) hop number is less that 14, and set to calculated hop number + X (1-2?) if it's higher than 14. Anyway we should use value 14 or less as much as possible to save extra byte.

Side note: Unfortunately Mesh Header is constructed in a way that Deep Hops Left is injected in the middle of the header, so when we forward a frame and the Hops Left is 15, then we can do memmove of payload to save one byte or just use Deep Hops Left until end (thoughts?)

issue_title: Upscore implementation may not perform as intended.
issue_body:
It appears as the upscore layer is intended to upscore a feature map.  Similiar to how the Deconv and Bilinear keral works in Caffe.

http://caffe.berkeleyvision.org/doxygen/classcaffe_1_1BilinearFiller.html

Their implementation mentions that the usage of Deconv (i.e. conv2d_transpose + bilinear kernel) should be the same as performing the following on an input image

```
import skimage.transform
plt.imshow(skimage.transform.rescale(image[:,:,0], 2, mode='constant', cval=0))
```

If the upscore implementation is intended to behave in a similar way, it does not.  Passing an image through this layer to upsample to twice the size results in noise.

The reason for this is that for an input with C_i channels, output with C_o channels, and stride of S, the layer creates a kernel of size [S,S,C_i,C_o]

This successfully works with tensorflows conv2d_transpose function, however as a result the kernal does not bi-linear filter each channel of the input to create an output, instead it sums over all channels of the input to create one of the output channels.   

In order to make this layer work properly, the input object should be unpacked along the channels dimension.  Each channel should be conv2d_transposed with a kernel of size [S,S,1, 1] and then the resulting maps should be packed back into an output of size [B H W C_o]

** Note **

This is not a very elegant solution due to the unpacking and packing, but it is unclear how to get this functionality out of the tf.conv2d_transpose function.

I couldn't find a way to do this in TFLearn without going a very long way around it. But being able to freeze weights on specific layers in a pre trained network would be great. If there is some way to do this already let me know lol.

For example using a pretrained VGG16 network you would want the weights in the first 4 convo layers to stay static(Froze) and then fine to the content layer 5. 

Then you would want to proceed to train your fully connected layers. 

If you update the weights on the entire thing you will be wrecking the weights on the first four layers or the meat of the vgg16 network that you want to use..  

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/17280185/23615983/dab3f304-024d-11e7-82b5-3a899f5d35b1.png)

issue_title: gcc8 emits -Wcast-function-type when compiling any module with a function binding.
issue_body:
## Issue description

gcc8 emits -Wcast-function-type (enabled by -Wextra) when compiling any module with a function binding.

## Reproducible example code

Start with pybind11/python_example, replace `src/main.cpp`'s contents with
```
#include <pybind11/pybind11.h>

namespace py = pybind11;

PYBIND11_MODULE(python_example, m) {
    m.def("foo", []() -> void {});
}
```
and compile with gcc8 and CFLAGS=-Wextra.  One gets
```
running build
running build_ext
gcc -pthread -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -Wextra -fPIC -I/usr/include/python3.6m -c /tmp/tmpk61gqk0m.cpp -o tmp/tmpk61gqk0m.o -std=c++14
cc1plus: warning: command line option ‘-Wstrict-prototypes’ is valid for C/ObjC but not for C++
gcc -pthread -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -Wextra -fPIC -I/usr/include/python3.6m -c /tmp/tmpre7j64d4.cpp -o tmp/tmpre7j64d4.o -fvisibility=hidden
cc1plus: warning: command line option ‘-Wstrict-prototypes’ is valid for C/ObjC but not for C++
building 'python_example' extension
gcc -pthread -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -Wextra -fPIC -I/usr/include/python3.6m -I/home/antony/.local/include/python3.6m -I/usr/include/python3.6m -c src/main.cpp -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.6/src/main.o -DVERSION_INFO="0.0.1" -std=c++14 -fvisibility=hidden
cc1plus: warning: command line option ‘-Wstrict-prototypes’ is valid for C/ObjC but not for C++
In file included from src/main.cpp:1:
/usr/include/python3.6m/pybind11/pybind11.h: In member function ‘void pybind11::cpp_function::initialize_generic(pybind11::detail::function_record*, const char*, const std::type_info* const*, pybind11::size_t)’:
/usr/include/python3.6m/pybind11/pybind11.h:317:74: warning: cast between incompatible function types from ‘PyObject* (*)(PyObject*, PyObject*, PyObject*)’ {aka ‘_object* (*)(_object*, _object*, _object*)’} to ‘PyCFunction’ {aka ‘_object* (*)(_object*, _object*)’} [-Wcast-function-type]
             rec->def->ml_meth = reinterpret_cast<PyCFunction>(*dispatcher);
                                                                          ^
g++ -pthread -shared -Wl,-O1,--sort-common,--as-needed,-z,relro,-z,now -Wl,-O1,--sort-common,--as-needed,-z,relro,-z,now -Wextra build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.6/src/main.o -L/usr/lib -lpython3.6m -o build/lib.linux-x86_64-3.6/python_example.cpython-36m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so
copying build/lib.linux-x86_64-3.6/python_example.cpython-36m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so ->
```

It looks like CPython is solving this by casting to a `void*` before casting to PyCFunction (https://bugs.python.org/issue33012 and PRs linked therein).

Note: everything below is using python 2.7 and the latest pull from pybind11/master.

I am not sure if this is meant to work, and I searched the open and closed issues for this, and didn't find anything obviously related.

#### Issue description

I modified the pickling test code to attempt to dump/load to/from a file.  I added this block of code to tests/test_pickling.py:

issue_title: Eventual collisions between opaque STL containers
issue_body:
After some chatting on gitter, @jagerman suggested that I raise an issue here, mostly so that it can be kept track of.

This is related to #439, but has more to do with what happens in the long run when more and more packages are developed using pybind11.

Currently,  opaque STL containers are most often generated with a combo of the `PYBIND11_MAKE_OPAQUE` macro and a call to `py::bind_vector`.

If two different modules by different developers generate the same opaque containers, an import error occurs if both modules are used together.  One simple solution is to wrap the binding in try/catch blocks as shown here: https://github.com/molpopgen/opaque_test.  (That example uses ccpimport to compile the code.)

However, the try/catch solution is imperfect: the name seen on the Python side depends on the import order.  Further, there is the deeper problem that `py::bind_vector` may optionally be invoked with `py::buffer_protocol()` and that the class type returned may be further augmented via `.defs` in the usual way.  The possible result then is that both the name and features/behavior depend on import order, which seems less than ideal.

The gitter discussion ended with the agreement that a solution is needed and that it is not clear what that solution should look like.

First, I would like to thank everyone who's been working really hard to provide us free SSL.

I'm not really sure how certbot is doing it's validation, but I bet it's simply relying to how the machine digs on the target.

Thanks!

issue_title: Error: urn:acme:error:malformed :: The request message was malformed :: Unable to find authorization
Better handle redirected HTTPS ports in Apache
issue_body: I couldn't find an existing issue for this, but I know we've had trouble here in the past and there was a report of the problem at https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/redirected-ports-break-certbot-apache/33385. We should make sure we handle this properly if a user sets `--tls-sni-01-port` to a different value.

I was looking at issues on the nginxparser project and found this one:

https://github.com/fatiherikli/nginxparser/issues/19

It appears to me that we can't parse configurations containing this. I got something like

`ParseException: Expected stringEnd (at char 100), (line:5, col:1)`

when trying to `nginxparser.loads()` a file that included that map directive.

cc @pde

issue_title: Pointing to a filepath that does not have an extension just downloads the file
issue_body: If users create an nbgitpuller link to a file that has no extension (e.g. `myfile`) then the "redirect" step will trigger the browser to simply download the file, and remain stuck on the nbgitpuller loading page. We should ensure that nbgitpuller at least always tries to open the file with the text editor if it isn't `ipynb`.

issue_title: Package fails to install on systems without `pkg-config`
issue_body:
On systems without `pkg-config`, `pip install lz4` fails with the following output:

```
Collecting lz4 (from ***)
  Downloading lz4-0.8.1.tar.gz
    Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 20, in <module>
      File "/tmp/pip-build-D9kUKL/lz4/setup.py", line 19, in <module>
        liblz4_found = subprocess.call(cmd) == 0
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 522, in call
        return Popen(*popenargs, **kwargs).wait()
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 710, in __init__
        errread, errwrite)
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 1327, in _execute_child
        raise child_exception
    OSError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory
```

I've investigated this a little bit, and it seems like the following chunk of code on the [setup.py file][setuppy] isn't handling all exceptional cases correctly:

```
# Check to see if we have a lz4 library installed on the system and
# use it if so. If not, we'll use the bundled library. If lz4 is
# installed it will have a pkg-config file, so we'll use pkg-config to
# check for existence of the library.
pkg_config_exe = os.environ.get('PKG_CONFIG', None) or 'pkg-config'
cmd = '{0} --exists liblz4'.format(pkg_config_exe).split()
liblz4_found = subprocess.call(cmd) == 0
```

In this specific case, the `pkg-config` binary doesn't exist on the system, making `subprocess.call()` fail with an `OSError` exception instead of a non-zero return code, and this exception isn't being handled by the code, making the installation fail.

This can be pretty common on minimal installations. In this particular situation, I'm running pip inside an ubuntu docker container, and it seems like the default docker ubuntu image doesn't contain `pkg-config` by default:

```
$ sudo docker run --rm -it ubuntu bash
root@f8481c78cd7b:/# pkg-config
bash: pkg-config: command not found
```

In my case I think I'll try to solve this by installing the `pkg-config` package, but since this might trip other people I've also decide to open this issue.

[setuppy]: https://github.com/python-lz4/python-lz4/blob/7dc8ce69a98fb63a9a6a92a25179d942b6082dfd/setup.py#L19

issue_title: Implement global channel manager contract per token.
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Part 1/2 of https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/1025.

>At the moment whenever we open a channel we create a smart contract per channel. We went with this implementation for simplicity reasons in the beginning but it is not optimal as it increases gas costs for channel opening due to the CREATE operation.


## Tasklist

- [ ] Implement the new global channel manager contract per token
- [ ] Write tests for the new token channel manager

## Problem Definition

At the moment, RaidenService is not covered by tests. This issue is created to have tests created for the service so that changes are tested.

`MatrixTransport::_send_queued_messages()` is supposed to send queued messages on startup, but since contents of the queue are raiden events (`raiden.transfer.mediated_transfer.events`) and they are transformed directly to a `raiden.message` without being signed, the signature in an outgoing message is set to `0x`.
As a result, partner will refuse to process such message.

## Problem Definition

Related to #1793

We're currently changing gevent's `SYSTEM_ERROR` setting to `(BaseException,)` in order to have greenlet exceptions be fatal and exit the program.

Unfortunately, this breaks (amongst others) the greenlet methods `.link()` and `.link_exception()` in case a greenlet throws an exception.

We need to figure out a way to preserve the unexpected-exceptions-exit-raiden behaviour without breaking other parts of the API.

issue_title: Rerun of completed tasks with missing output
issue_body:
A seeming simple scenario: Run a script with task, task completed. Remove output files, re-run, and sos will find that task already completed. In this case,

1. `sos run -s default` will still check signature and reaches the `task` statement because of signature mismatch.
2. underlying `sos execute task -s default` will refuse to run a completed task.
3. `sos run -s force` will rerun the step and task, but innocent steps/tasks will also be rerun.

This is a new problem with the new task format because previously sos has a `signature-mismatch` status for tasks. That is to say, when we check if a task is completed, it will check if the signature matches, so `sos execute task -s default` will execute if the signature mismatch.

Now, before we restore this `signature-mismatch` status, we need to think what this status means.

This status essentially binds the `task` file with its outputs, so a task would be considered failed whenever the input or output files are moved or removed. Considering you have a large number of jobs on the cluster, it is almost inevitable that input output files will be moved around after jobs are completed. 

There are also technical difficulties in getting this status. Because input and output files are stored outside of task files. We will have to read a task file, get its signature, and check input output files, potentially even calculate md5 of all these files. Considering doing this for ten thousand jobs that you have done in the past month each time you check the status of jobs? We can of course cache the status but we still need to check a lot of files to at least see if their timestamp has been change between each `sos status` check.

Now related to #1015 if we are re-executing a step with lots of jobs, jobs with now removed output files will be rerun, which is not acceptable in many cases.

So we are somehow stuck here with #1015...

Up to now, we compose different transforms via `Compose` transform, which works like `nn.Sequential` but for transforms. Each transform is applied independently to `input` and `target`.
While simple and efficient, there has been an increasing demand on extending the available transforms to accept both `input` and `target`, see #9, #115, #221 for some examples. This would allow performing the same random transformations in both the `input` and the `target`, for tasks such as semantic segmentation.

There are a few possible approaches and I'll summarize the ones that have been mentioned in here:
1. provide a set of random transforms working on pairs (or triplets, etc) of images, as proposed for example in #221. There are a couple of downsides of this approach: (a) it doesn't scale when we want to combine images coming from different domains (in object detection, we have image and bounding boxes for example, and we need to reimplement a new transform for each pair, introducing some redundancy in the code), (b) hard-coded to work with 2 inputs (but could be extended to `n`-inputs without difficulties)
2. factor out the randomness in the transforms, so that we can use the same functionality we currently have, just needing to add a `generate_seed` call in `__getitem__`, as was proposed in #115 . The drawback from this approach is that in some cases, we need the information of `input` to be able to perform the transformation in `target` (imagine flipping a bounding box horizontally, it requires the image width, which is not available right away).
3. provide a generic base class that samples the random transformation parameters, and passes those parameters to the input arguments, as proposed by @chsasank in [the slack channel](https://pytorch.slackarchive.io/vision/page-2/ts-1501788713664208). While this is an improvement over both #221 and #115, it still suffers from the same limitation of #115 as we still only pass one input argument at a time.

Also, all those options actually handle each input independently. Furthermore, for both 1. and 3., the order of the operations is fixed in the dataset class (first input transforms, then target transforms, then joint transforms, for example). There might be cases where it would be convenient to not be restricted to such orderings, and alternate between single transform and joint transform.

One possibility to address those issues would be to provide a set of functions like `nn.Split`, `nn.Concat`, `nn.Select`, etc, so that we can always pass all inputs to the transforms, and let the transforms be implemented by the user explicitly. This mimics the legacy `nn` behavior.
The downside of this approach is that it gets very complicated to write some transformations, and this doesn't buy us much.

Instead, one simpler approach (which has already been advocated in the past by @colesbury) is to let the user directly subclass the dataset and implement their complex transforms in there. This approach (4.) would look something like
```python
class VOCDatasetSegmentation(VOCDataset):
    def __init__(self, flip=False, **kwargs):
        super(VOCDatasetSegmentation, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self.flip = flip

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        image, target = super(VOCDatasetSegmentation, self).__getitem__(idx)
        do_flip = np.random.random() > 0.5
        if self.flip and do_flip:
            # flip image and bbox here
        return image, target
```
A downside is that we can't easily re-use those transforms in a different Dataset class (COCO for example).

The question now is if there is an intermediate design that we could leverage that keeps the simplicity of 4., without having to subclass the dataset and reimplement every time the same transforms?

What about yet another possibility (5.) would be to let the user write the code of their as follows

```python
class Dataset(object):
    def __init__(self, transforms=None):
        self.transforms = transforms
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        # get image1, image2, bounding_box
        # the transforms takes all inputs into account
        if self.transforms:
            image1, image2, bounding_box = self.transforms(image1, image2, bounding_box)
        return image1, image2, bounding_box

from torchvision.transforms import random_horizontal_flip

class MyJointRandomFlipTransform(object):
    def __call__(self, image1, image2, bounding_box):
        # provide a functional interface for the current transforms
        # so that they can be easily reused, and have the parameters
        # of the transformation if needed
        image1, params = random_horizontal_flip(image1, return_params=True)
        # reuses the same transformations, if wanted
        image2 = random_horizontal_flip(image2, params=params)
        # no transformation in torchvision for bounding_box, have to do it
        # ourselves
        if params.flip:
            bounding_box[:, 1] = image1.size(2) - bounding_box[:, 1]
            bounding_box[:, 3] = image1.size(2) - bounding_box[:, 3]
        return image1, image2, bounding_box
```

In this way, we have the flexibility subclassing the dataset, while being more modular and easy to implement.
There would be some differences in the way we write our datasets currently, but we could have a fallback implementation for backward compatibility, in the lines of
```python
class StandardTransform(object):
    def __init__(self, transform, target_transform):
        self.transform = transform
        self.target_transform = target_transform

   def __call__(self, input, target):
        if self.transform:
            input = self.transform(input)
        if self.target_transform:
            target = self.target_transform(target)
        return input, target
```
and we would replace in the current datasets `transform` and `target_transform` by a single `transforms`, while keeping the old behavior

```python
class Dataset(object):
    def __init__(self, path, transforms=None, transform=None, target_transform=None):
        # assert that only transforms or (transform, target_transform) can be set at a time
        if transforms is None:
           transforms = StandardTransform(transform, target_transform)
        self.transforms = transforms

    # getitem only uses transforms from now on
```

What do you think? Do you see drawbacks on using such an approach?

issue_title: Actors cannot be created before ray.init is called.
issue_body:
It's important to be able to define remote functions and things like that before a call to `ray.init` (for example, these may be defined in a Python module which are imported at the top of the file before you call `ray.init`).

The same is probably true for actors. We should enable actors to be defined before a call to `ray.init` by caching the created actors and exporting them when `ray.init` is eventually called.

issue_title: Global scheduler load balances poorly between machines with different numbers of CPUs or GPUs.
issue_body:
Suppose we start two machines with very different numbers of CPUs.

```
ray start --head --num-cpus=16 --redis-port=6379
```

```
ray start --redis-address=<head-node-ip>:6379 --num-cpus=1
```

Then we run the following driver script.

```python
import ray
import time

ray.init(redis_address="<head-node-ip>:6379")

@ray.remote
def f():
    time.sleep(0.01)

%time l = ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(1000)])
```

This takes a little over 5 seconds, but it should only take 1000 * 0.01 / 17 = 0.588 seconds.

5 seconds comes from the fact that half of the tasks are getting assigned to the machine with only CPU.

This can be verified by looking at

```python
tasks = ray.global_state.task_table()
local_schedulers = [v["LocalSchedulerID"] for k, v in tasks.items()]
print([(ls, local_schedulers.count(ls)) for ls in set(local_schedulers)])
```

This prints

```
[('ffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffff', 1), ('4fa3cce970a8af9f28cf8234314854a8f9904d7d', 492), ('029251931d69065c365024283f3eb78cebb7895d', 508)]
```

The one with all `f`'s can be ignored.

cc @atumanov and @Wapaul1

issue_body:
### Expected behaviour
Contiguous masking bit should mask data in case of no data found.

### Actual behaviour
It is not masking data even though red, green and blue bands have no data but nir band has data.
This can be tested on LS7 dataset dated 2002/12/17

### Steps to reproduce the behaviour
This is the code that can be used to check masking bit.

import datacube
import fiona
from datacube.api import make_mask
from datacube.utils.geometry import CRS, GeoBox, Geometry
from datacube.utils import geometry
import numpy as np

filepath = '/g/data/r78/intertidal/GA_tidal_model.shp'

mask_spec=dict()

mask_spec['flags'] = { 'contiguous': True,
          'cloud_acca': 'no_cloud',
          'cloud_fmask': 'no_cloud',
          'cloud_shadow_acca': 'no_cloud_shadow',
          'cloud_shadow_fmask': 'no_cloud_shadow',
          'blue_saturated': False,
          'green_saturated': False,
          'red_saturated': False,
          'nir_saturated': False,
          'swir1_saturated': False,
          'swir2_saturated': False
          }

def pq_fuser(dest, src):
    valid_bit = 8
    valid_val = (1 << valid_bit)
    no_data_dest_mask = ~(dest & valid_val).astype(bool)
    np.copyto(dest, src, where=no_data_dest_mask)
    both_data_mask = (valid_val & dest & src).astype(bool)
    np.copyto(dest, src & dest, where=both_data_mask)

dc=datacube.Datacube(app='test')

with fiona.open(filepath) as Input:
    crs = CRS(str(Input.crs_wkt))
    for feature in Input:
        if feature['properties']['ID'] == 136:
            geom=feature['geometry']
            boundary_polygon = Geometry(geom, crs)
            #print (feature)
            break
dt1='2002-12-17 00:00:00'
dt2='2002-12-17 23:59:00'
indexers={'geopolygon': boundary_polygon, 'group_by':'solar_day'}
pq = dc.load(product='ls7_pq_albers', time=(dt1, dt2), fuse_func=pq_fuser, **indexers)
data = dc.load(product='ls7_nbar_albers', time=(dt1, dt2), measurements=['red', 'green', 'blue', 'nir'], **indexers)
mask = make_mask(pq, **mask_spec['flags'])
data = data.where(mask['pixelquality'])
print (data.green)
print (data.nir)

issue_title: Should we actually run fixtures concurrently?
issue_body:
Upsides:

* Can make test setup/teardown faster
* Since we have to run fixtures in independent tasks anyway (see #55), running them concurrently was easier than running them sequentially.

Counterarguments:

* Might increase the chance of flaky tests, race conditions, etc.
  * In particular, race conditions involving `ContextVars` (see https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/pull/50#issuecomment-414648141) – currently fixtures all share a `contextvars.Context`, so if multiple fixtures are mutating the same `ContextVar`, the results will be random. (Of course, doing this is inherently nonsensical. But nonsensical-and-consistent is still better than nonsensical-and-random.)
* Might make failing tests harder to debug
* Is there really that much concurrency to extract from fixture setup? How many people use multiple independent fixtures that need to block for significant amounts of time?
* Running them sequentially isn't actually that hard, if we let pytest pick the order for us (see https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/pull/50#issuecomment-414652134)

Leaving this issue open for now for discussion, and to collect any positive/negative experiences people want to report.

With the recent changes to the DataFuture behavior and the File based model that we are moving to in general, we want to have File behave as closely to old behavior as possible so that there's least amount of code breakage. This is one such case:

Earlier we could do this since the result of a DataFuture was a string
> f = open(DataFuture1.result(), 'r') 

Now the result of a DataFuture is a File, which can't simply be passed to open:
> f = open(File, 'r') <-- Fails 

This can be fixed by making File follow the [os.PathLike](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.PathLike) interface

Not sure if I'm doing something wrong  or missing something daft, but when I attempted to set up a static website on s3 and request the website, i get the html page rendered a xml in chrome. Is it because the Content-Type is application/xml? 

Expected behaviour
Using Chrome browse to http://192.168.99.100:4572/TestBucket/index.html returns my webpage rendered correctly

Steps to reproduce
from a working directory containing my index.html page.
aws --endpoint-url=http://192.168.99.100:4572 s3api create-bucket --bucket TestBucket
aws --endpoint-url=http://192.168.99.100:4752 s3 sync . s3://TestBucket --acl public-read
aws --endpoint-url=http://192.168.99.100:4752 s3 website s3://TestBucket --index-document index.html

issue_body:

I'm trying to configure Mattermost notification service and it keeps throwing me error 400 `Bad Request`.

```
    service = project.services.get('mattermost')
    service.webhook = webhook_url
    service.save()
```

```
    raise error(e.error_message, e.response_code, e.response_body)
gitlab.exceptions.GitlabUpdateError: 400: 400 Bad Request
```

Testing with curl, it also doesn't work if I don't explicitly set header to `application/json`:
```
$ curl -v --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: xxxx-uti" -X PUT https://example.tld/api/v4/projects/7812837/services/mattermost -d '{"webhook":"https://mattermost.host/"}'
{"error":"webhook is missing"}
```

But it works when I set the header:
```
curl --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: xxxx-uti" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X PUT https://example.tld/api/v4/projects/7812837/services/mattermost -d '{"webhook":"https://mattermost.host/"}'
```

issue_title: moban should report the directory is missing
issue_body:
Running moban on an uninitialised `pyexcel/pyexcel` clone:

`OSError: Both pyexcel.yaml and commons/config/pyexcel.yaml does not exist`

This is a little confusing.
It would be better to identify that `commons` doesnt exist.
And per https://github.com/moremoban/moban/issues/6 , moban should find it automatically!

Also `pyexcel.yml` exists, which may cause the user to wonder if that is related to the problem.

Scoring functions are an important aspect of variant scoring and should be made as explicit as possible.

Suggestions:
- Move `scoring_fns.py` to `kipoi/postprocessing/variant_effects/scores.py`
- Rename to `Pred_analysis` to `Score`
- Rename to `Rc_merging_pred_analysis` to `RCScore`
- add the string->RCScore mapping  https://github.com/kipoi/kipoi/blob/master/kipoi/cli/postproc.py#L23-L43

issue_title: Update README with the new status of django-fiber
issue_body:
Now issue #244 has been resolved the first section of the README should be updated explaining the status of this project.

Suggestions, commits or pull requests are welcome ;-).

For django > 1.10, the middleware should probably use the  django.utils.deprecation.MiddlewareMixin mixin (https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/http/middleware/#upgrading-middleware) as MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES are deprecated, correct ?

Once the data is in s3 and dynamo, we are not clear about how the prediction flow will work. We are assuming that the user will setup the model server themselves. There needs to be an intermediary that takes in a user ID, queries dynamo etc for the features and then queries the model server. We need to think through how a user will set this up and what can we do to make this bit easier

A `GraphProro` contains inputs and outputs in terms of `ValueInfoProto`. Is it necessary to specify tensor dimensions in those `ValueInfoProto`? Loop is a dynamic computation, so the output shape is usually unknown.

issue_title: auto_pad in conv with stride != 1 not specified
issue_body:
The spec for Conv
https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#Conv
does not specify what to do when stride is greater than 1.
Should it behave the same as MaxPool
https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#MaxPool
where stride == 2 => output size is floor((input size)/2) ?

Would be good to describe this, for completeness sake.

issue_title: Spec clarity: contradiction on domain names

issue_body:
Please confirm for LRN: 
Normalization is pixelwise across channels ( as per Tensorflow ) and is not within-channel in spatial rectangle. That is, it is like Caffe LRN with ACROSS_CHANNELS = 1.
The onnx info for LRN is  https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#LRN

issue_title: implement a helper script for single machine multi-GPU training
issue_body: For single machine multi-GPU training, we need to launch multiple processes, with `TF_CONFIG` properly set for each process. We should implement a helper script which could do this automatically.

issue_title: 用notebook绘很多图 jupyter-notebook 用户注意了： jupyter v0.5.0+ 需要这个设置
issue_body:
这个适用于用jupyter画多图，等画多了之后，有些图不显示的用户。

这个时候看jupyter-notebook console output， 有没有
```
IOPub data rate exceeded.
    The notebook server will temporarily stop sending output
    to the client in order to avoid crashing it.
    To change this limit, set the config variable
    `--NotebookApp.iopub_data_rate_limit`.
```

然后：
`jupyter notebook --generate-config`
`vi ~/.jupyter/jupyter_notebook_config.py`

然后：

```
## (bytes/sec) Maximum rate at which messages can be sent on iopub before they
#  are limited.
c.NotebookApp.iopub_data_rate_limit = 10000000
```

参考：
https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/2287

issue_title: should convert new line into white space
issue_body:
When using:

    accomplishment: >
      Mark set a major league
      home run record in 1998.

the result should be:

    { accomplishment: 'Mark set a major league home run record in 1998.' }

not:

    { accomplishment: 'Mark set a major leaguehome run record in 1998.' }

The > should convert new lines into white spaces according to the specification: http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2760844

issue_title: foldline doctest should not test Plone foldline
issue_body:
```python
def foldline(text, lenght=75, newline='\r\n'):
    """ Make a string folded per RFC5545 (each line must be less than 75 octets)

    >>> from plone.event.utils import foldline
    >>> foldline('foo')
    u'foo\\r\\n'
```

Three of the four foldline unit tests fail.

```bash
$ python -c "import doctest, parser ; doctest.testmod(parser)"
...
3 items had failures:
   4 of  50 in parser.Contentline
   1 of   7 in parser.Contentlines
   3 of   4 in parser.foldline

```

I tried to run  the virtualenv example:

https://github.com/paver/paver/blob/master/docs/source/virtualenv.rst

```python
from paver.easy import task
from paver.virtual import virtualenv

@task
@virtualenv(dir="virtualenv")
    def t1():
        import some_module_existing_only_in_virtualenv
```

First I got `IndentationError`, I fixed it, then I got: `TypeError: 'module' object is not callable`

Paver 1.0.5

Python 2.6.6
